verses.flow: The book of the genesis of Jesus Christ, Son of David, Son of Abraham.
verses.note: GREEK: "Bíblos genéseōs" (βίβλος γενέσεως) echoes the LXX phrase found in Gen 2:4 and 5:1, where it introduces a "book of origins" or "book of becoming." The term génesis (G1078) carries overtones of origin, birth, and creation. TYPOLOGY: By opening with this phrase, Matthew deliberately frames Jesus as the culmination of the entire story of Israel — a new Genesis. The dual title "Son of David, Son of Abraham" establishes Jesus as both the promised King of the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:12-16) and the Seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed (Gen 12:3; Gal 3:16).

verses.flow: Abraham begot Isaac, and Isaac begot Jacob, and Jacob begot Judah and his brothers,
and Judah begot Perez and Zerah from Tamar, and Perez begot Hezron, and Hezron begot Aram,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Tamar is the first of four women named in the genealogy (along with Rahab, Ruth, and the wife of Uriah). Each of these women represents an irregular or scandalous union by conventional standards, yet each was instrumental in the messianic line. TYPOLOGY: Their inclusion foreshadows the unexpected means by which God accomplishes His redemptive purposes, culminating in the virgin birth itself. Tamar's story (Gen 38) demonstrates that God's covenant faithfulness persists even through human failure.

verses.flow: and Aram begot Aminadab, and Aminadab begot Nahshon, and Nahshon begot Salmon,
and Salmon begot Boaz from Rahab, and Boaz begot Obed from Ruth, and Obed begot Jesse,
and Jesse begot David the king. And David begot Solomon from the wife of Uriah,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Matthew pointedly identifies David as "the king" (τὸν βασιλέα, ton basilea), the only figure in the genealogy given this title. This emphasizes the royal, Davidic lineage of Jesus the Christ. GREEK: The phrase "from the wife of Uriah" (ἐκ τῆς τοῦ Οὐρίου) deliberately avoids naming Bathsheba, keeping the focus on the sin of David and the cost to Uriah — a Hittite faithful to Israel's God. Matthew refuses to sanitize the messianic line.

verses.flow: and Solomon begot Rehoboam, and Rehoboam begot Abijah, and Abijah begot Asaph,
and Asaph begot Jehoshaphat, and Jehoshaphat begot Joram, and Joram begot Uzziah,
and Uzziah begot Jotham, and Jotham begot Ahaz, and Ahaz begot Hezekiah,
and Hezekiah begot Manasseh, and Manasseh begot Amos, and Amos begot Josiah,
and Josiah begot Jechoniah and his brothers, at the time of the deportation to Babylon.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Metoikesía" (μετοικεσία, G3350) means "deportation" or "forced resettlement," referring to the Babylonian exile of 586 BC. This term is distinct from mere migration; it denotes a coerced removal. STRUCTURE: The exile marks the second major structural hinge in Matthew's threefold division of fourteen generations (v.17), representing the nadir of Israel's history — the loss of land, temple, and Davidic throne — from which the Christ emerges as restorer.

verses.flow: And after the deportation to Babylon, Jechoniah begot Shealtiel, and Shealtiel begot Zerubbabel,
and Zerubbabel begot Abiud, and Abiud begot Eliakim, and Eliakim begot Azor,
and Azor begot Zadok, and Zadok begot Achim, and Achim begot Eliud,
and Eliud begot Eleazar, and Eleazar begot Matthan, and Matthan begot Jacob,

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and Jacob begot Joseph the husband of Mary, from whom was born Jesus, the one called Christ.
verses.note: GREEK: The genealogical formula shifts critically here. Throughout vv.2-15, the active verb "begot" (ἐγέννησεν) is used of each father. Here Matthew writes "from whom" (ἐξ ἧς, ex hēs — feminine singular), indicating Jesus was born of Mary, not begotten by Joseph. The passive ἐγεννήθη ("was born/was begotten") signals divine agency. THEOLOGICAL: Joseph is the legal father, securing Jesus' Davidic claim, but not the biological father — preserving the virgin birth doctrine.

verses.flow: Therefore all the generations from Abraham until David were fourteen generations, and from David until the deportation to Babylon fourteen generations, and from the deportation to Babylon until the Christ fourteen generations.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The three sets of fourteen generations (3 × 14) are likely structured around the numerical value (gematria) of David's name in Hebrew (דוד = 4+6+4 = 14). This is a deliberate literary and theological device, not merely a historical accident. Matthew may omit certain kings (cf. 1 Chr 3:11-12) to achieve this symbolic structure, emphasizing that Jesus is the divinely ordained climax of Israel's entire covenantal history.

verses.flow: Now the genesis of Jesus Christ was thus: His mother Mary having been betrothed to Joseph, before they came together, she was found to be with child from the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: GREEK: "Génesis" (γένεσις) appears again, now referring specifically to the origin-event of Jesus' birth. "Mnēsteutheísēs" (μνηστευθείσης, G3423) indicates formal betrothal, which in Jewish law constituted a legally binding marriage contract, though the couple had not yet cohabited. "Ek pneúmatos hagíou" (ἐκ πνεύματος ἁγίου) — "from the Holy Spirit" — identifies the divine source of conception. THEOLOGICAL: The Spirit's creative role here echoes Gen 1:2, where the Spirit hovers over creation — Jesus' birth is a new creative act of God.

verses.flow: And Joseph her husband, being a righteous man and not willing to make a public example of her, purposed to divorce her secretly.

verses.flow: But as he pondered these things, behold, an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream, saying, "Joseph, son of David, do not fear to take Mary as your wife, for that which has been begotten in her is from the Holy Spirit.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Ángelos kyríou" (ἄγγελος κυρίου) — "angel of the Lord" — recalls the mal'ak YHWH of the Hebrew Bible, a divine messenger who operates as God's authoritative representative (cf. Gen 16:7; Exod 3:2; Judg 13:3). In Second Temple angelology, this figure mediates divine decrees to humanity. TYPOLOGY: Joseph as "son of David" receiving divine revelation in dreams parallels the patriarch Joseph (Gen 37-50), who also received divine communication through dreams and preserved the covenant family.

verses.flow: And she shall bear a Son, and you shall call His name Jesus, for He shall save His people from their sins."
verses.note: GREEK: "Iēsous" (Ἰησοῦς) is the Greek form of the Hebrew "Yehoshua" (יהושע), meaning "YHWH saves" or "YHWH is salvation." The angel provides an explicit etymology: "for He (autós, emphatic) shall save (sṓsei, σώσει, G4982) His people from their sins." THEOLOGICAL: This is a programmatic statement for the entire Gospel — Jesus' mission is fundamentally soteriological. "His people" (τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ) identifies Jesus as possessing a people, an inherently divine prerogative (cf. Ps 130:8; Ezek 37:23).

verses.flow: Now all this came to pass so that what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet might be fulfilled, saying,

verses.flow: "Behold, the virgin shall be with child and shall bear a Son, and they shall call His name Emmanuel," which is, being translated, "God with us."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct quotation of Isa 7:14 from the LXX, which uses "parthénos" (παρθένος, G3933) — "virgin" — translating the Hebrew "almah." Matthew identifies this Isaianic sign as reaching its ultimate fulfillment in Mary's virginal conception. THEOLOGICAL: "Emmanuel" (Ἐμμανουήλ) — "God with us" — is not merely a symbolic name but a theological declaration that in Jesus, God Himself has come to dwell with His people. This theme brackets Matthew's Gospel, echoed in 28:20: "I am with you always." DIVINE_COUNCIL: The incarnation represents God entering the human realm in an unprecedented manner — the Most High God taking on flesh among the beings He created.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Joseph, having been raised from sleep, did as the angel of the Lord commanded him, and he took his wife,
and he did not know her until she bore a Son, and he called His name Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ouk egínōsken" (οὐκ ἐγίνωσκεν) — "he did not know" — uses ginṓskō (G1097) in the Hebraic sense of sexual intimacy (cf. Gen 4:1 LXX). "Héōs hoû" (ἕως οὗ) — "until" — indicates the period of abstinence prior to the birth, affirming both the virgin birth and the reality of the marriage. THEOLOGICAL: Joseph's obedience in naming the child (kalésen, "he called") legally constitutes Jesus as his son, securing the Davidic lineage through legal paternity while preserving the miracle of the virgin conception.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: Now when Jesus had been born in Bethlehem of Judea in the days of Herod the king, behold, magi from the east came to Jerusalem,
verses.note: GREEK: "Magi" (μάγοι, magoi, Strong's G3097) refers not to sorcerers but to a priestly-scholarly class from Persia or Babylon, skilled in astronomy and dream interpretation. "From the east" (ἀπὸ ἀνατολῶν) literally "from the risings," i.e., where the sun rises. HISTORICAL: Herod the Great reigned 37–4 BC; Jesus' birth is thus dated before 4 BC. Bethlehem was a small village about six miles south of Jerusalem.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: saying, "Where is He who has been born King of the Jews? For we saw His star in the east, and we have come to worship Him."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The magi seek to "worship" (προσκυνῆσαι, proskynesai, G4352) Him — the same word used for divine worship throughout the LXX and NT. Gentile seekers come to worship the Jewish King, foreshadowing the ingathering of the nations. TYPOLOGY: The star recalls Balaam's oracle: "A star shall come out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel" (Num 24:17).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And when Herod the king heard this, he was troubled, and all Jerusalem with him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having gathered together all the chief priests and scribes of the people, he was inquiring of them where the Christ was to be born.
verses.note: GREEK: "Christ" (ὁ Χριστός, ho Christos, G5547) is the Greek equivalent of Hebrew "Mashiach" (Anointed One). Herod's question reveals that the expected birthplace of the Messiah was a matter of established scribal knowledge. HISTORICAL: The "chief priests and scribes of the people" constituted the scholarly religious leadership of Second Temple Judaism.

verses.flow: And they said to him, "In Bethlehem of Judea, for thus it has been written through the prophet:

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: 'And you, Bethlehem, land of Judah, are by no means least among the rulers of Judah; for out of you shall come forth a ruler who shall shepherd My people Israel.'"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation combines Micah 5:2 and 2 Samuel 5:2, linking the Messiah's birthplace with the Davidic shepherd-king motif. The verb "shepherd" (ποιμανεῖ, poimanei, G4165) portrays the coming ruler as both king and caring shepherd over God's covenant people (cf. Ezek 34:23, Rev 2:27). THEOLOGICAL: Matthew demonstrates Jesus' fulfillment of messianic prophecy through geographic specificity.

verses.flow: And seeing the star, they rejoiced with exceedingly great joy.

verses.flow: And having come into the house, they saw the child with Mary His mother, and falling down they worshiped Him; and having opened their treasures, they offered to Him gifts: gold and frankincense and myrrh.
verses.note: GREEK: "Worshiped" (προσεκύνησαν, prosekynēsan) — they prostrated before the child, an act of royal and divine homage. "Treasures" (θησαυρούς, thēsaurous, G2344) refers to treasure chests or coffers. TYPOLOGY: Gold signifies kingship, frankincense (λίβανον, libanon, G3030) signifies priestly/divine worship, and myrrh (σμύρναν, smyrnan, G4666) signifies suffering and burial — prophetically encompassing Christ's offices and destiny (cf. Ps 72:10-15, Isa 60:6).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And having been divinely warned in a dream not to return to Herod, they departed to their own country by another way.
verses.note: GREEK: "Divinely warned" (χρηματισθέντες, chrēmatisthentes, G5537) indicates a divine oracle or revelation, not merely a natural dream. This word is used in the LXX and NT specifically for divine communication. DIVINE_COUNCIL: God sovereignly directs events through angelic and prophetic channels to protect the messianic child, overruling earthly powers.

verses.flow: Now when they had departed, behold, an angel of the Lord appears in a dream to Joseph, saying, "Arise and take the child and His mother, and flee to Egypt, and remain there until I tell you; for Herod is about to seek the child to destroy Him."

verses.flow: and was there until the death of Herod, so that what was spoken by the Lord through the prophet might be fulfilled, saying, "Out of Egypt I called My Son."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Hosea 11:1, which in its original context refers to Israel's exodus from Egypt. Matthew sees Jesus as the true Israel — the faithful Son who recapitulates Israel's journey. As Israel was God's "son" called out of Egypt, so the Son of God Himself is called out of Egypt (cf. Exod 4:22-23). THEOLOGICAL: This is a typological fulfillment rather than a predictive one; Christ embodies and fulfills Israel's story.

verses.flow: Then was fulfilled what was spoken through Jeremiah the prophet, saying,

verses.flow: "A voice was heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children, and she would not be comforted, because they are no more."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Jeremiah 31:15. Rachel, buried near Bethlehem (Gen 35:19), weeps for her descendants carried into exile. Matthew sees a second fulfillment in the slaughter of Bethlehem's children. Significantly, Jeremiah 31 continues with promises of restoration and the New Covenant (Jer 31:31-34), suggesting that even this tragedy is encompassed within God's redemptive purpose.

verses.flow: saying, "Arise and take the child and His mother, and go into the land of Israel, for those who were seeking the life of the child have died."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Those who were seeking the life of the child have died" closely echoes Exodus 4:19 — "for all the men who were seeking your life have died" — spoken to Moses. Matthew presents Jesus as a new and greater Moses, the ultimate deliverer of God's people (cf. Deut 18:15, Acts 3:22).

verses.flow: But having heard that Archelaus was reigning over Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there; and having been divinely warned in a dream, he departed into the regions of Galilee,

verses.flow: and he came and dwelt in a city called Nazareth, so that what was spoken through the prophets might be fulfilled, that "He shall be called a Nazarene."
verses.note: GREEK: "Nazarene" (Ναζωραῖος, Nazōraios, G3480) is a notable term. No single OT verse states "He shall be called a Nazarene," hence Matthew says "through the prophets" (plural). THEOLOGICAL: This likely involves a wordplay on the Hebrew "netzer" (נֵצֶר, "branch") from Isaiah 11:1, connecting Jesus to the messianic Branch, and perhaps also the lowly reputation of Nazareth (cf. John 1:46), reflecting the Servant's humble origins (Isa 53:2-3).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And in those days John the Baptist arrives, proclaiming in the wilderness of Judea,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and saying, "Repent, for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near."
verses.note: GREEK: "Metanoeite" (μετανοεῖτε, Strong's G3340) is a present active imperative, commanding ongoing repentance — a turning of the mind, not merely regret. "Engiken" (ἤγγικεν, perfect tense) indicates the kingdom has drawn near and remains in a state of nearness. THEOLOGICAL: "The kingdom of the heavens" (hē basileia tōn ouranōn) is a phrase unique to Matthew, likely reflecting Jewish reverence avoiding direct naming of God, parallel to "kingdom of God" in Mark and Luke.

verses.flow: For this is the one spoken of through Isaiah the prophet, saying, "A voice of one crying out in the wilderness: Prepare the way of the Lord, make straight His paths."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Isaiah 40:3 (LXX), originally describing the return from Babylonian exile as a new exodus. Matthew applies this typologically to John preparing Israel for the arrival of the Lord Himself — identifying Jesus with the "Kyrios" (Lord) whose way is being prepared. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In Isaiah's original context, the voice is a member of the heavenly council issuing a decree; Matthew sees this fulfilled in John's prophetic commission.

verses.flow: Now John himself had his garment of camel's hair and a leather belt around his waist, and his food was locusts and wild honey.
Then Jerusalem was going out to him, and all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan,

verses.flow: and they were being baptized in the Jordan River by him, confessing their sins.

verses.flow: But seeing many of the Pharisees and Sadducees coming to his baptism, he said to them, "Offspring of vipers! Who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?
verses.note: GREEK: "Gennēmata echidnōn" (γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν) — "offspring of vipers" — gennēma (G1081) denotes that which is begotten or produced, emphasizing nature and lineage. HISTORICAL: Pharisees and Sadducees rarely appeared together; their joint arrival signals the significance of John's movement. The "coming wrath" (mellousēs orgēs) reflects apocalyptic expectations widespread in Second Temple Judaism (cf. Mal 3:2; 4:1).

verses.flow: Produce therefore fruit worthy of repentance,
and do not presume to say among yourselves, 'We have Abraham as father,' for I say to you that God is able from these stones to raise up children to Abraham.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: John dismantles ethnic presumption of covenant security. The claim "We have Abraham as father" reflects a widespread Second Temple belief that Abrahamic descent guaranteed eschatological salvation. John asserts God's sovereign power to create a covenant people ex nihilo — from stones (lithōn), possibly a wordplay on the Aramaic "stones" (abnayya) and "children" (bnayya). (cf. Rom 9:6-8; Gal 3:7).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: And already the axe is laid to the root of the trees; every tree therefore not producing good fruit is cut down and cast into fire.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: I indeed baptize you in water unto repentance, but the one coming after me is mightier than I, whose sandals I am not worthy to carry. He will baptize you in the Holy Spirit and fire,
verses.note: GREEK: "Ischyroteros" (ἰσχυρότερος, G2478 comparative) — "mightier" — a term of overwhelming power. "Hypodemata bastasai" — to carry sandals was a task considered beneath even a Hebrew slave. THEOLOGICAL: The threefold baptism — water, Holy Spirit, and fire — distinguishes John's preparatory ministry from the Messiah's eschatological ministry of purification and judgment. The Spirit-and-fire baptism echoes Isaiah 4:4 and Malachi 3:2-3.

verses.flow: whose winnowing fork is in His hand, and He will thoroughly cleanse His threshing floor, and will gather His wheat into the storehouse, but the chaff He will burn up with unquenchable fire."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The threshing floor imagery draws on rich OT harvest-judgment typology (Isa 41:15-16; Jer 15:7; Dan 2:35). The "winnowing fork" (ptyon, G4425) separates wheat from chaff, depicting final eschatological judgment. THEOLOGICAL: "Unquenchable fire" (pyri asbestō) signifies the permanence and finality of divine judgment — a fire that cannot be extinguished by human effort (cf. Isa 66:24; Mark 9:43).

verses.flow: Then Jesus arrives from Galilee at the Jordan, to John, to be baptized by him.

verses.flow: But John was preventing Him, saying, "I have need to be baptized by You, and You come to me?"

verses.flow: But answering, Jesus said to him, "Permit it now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness." Then he permits Him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "To fulfill all righteousness" (plērōsai pasan dikaiosynēn) is a key Matthean phrase. Jesus submits to baptism not from personal need for repentance, but to identify with sinful humanity and to inaugurate His messianic mission in full obedience to the Father's righteous purposes. This "fulfillment" theme pervades Matthew (cf. Matt 5:17). GREEK: "Aphes" (ἄφες) is aorist imperative of aphiēmi — "permit" or "let go" — a firm yet gracious command.

verses.flow: And having been baptized, Jesus immediately went up from the water; and behold, the heavens were opened, and He saw the Spirit of God descending as a dove, coming upon Him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The opening of the heavens (ēneōchthēsan hoi ouranoi) signals a theophanic moment — a breach between the heavenly and earthly realms. The Spirit descending "as a dove" (hōsei peristeran) evokes Genesis 1:2 (Spirit hovering over waters) and signals new creation. TYPOLOGY: Jesus emerging from the waters recapitulates Israel's passage through the Red Sea and Jordan, beginning a new exodus as the true Israel (cf. Exod 14; Josh 3).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And behold, a voice out of the heavens, saying, "This is My Son, the Beloved, in whom I am well pleased."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The heavenly voice combines three OT texts: "My Son" echoes Psalm 2:7 (royal messianic enthronement), "the Beloved" echoes Genesis 22:2 (Isaac as the beloved son — sacrifice typology), and "in whom I am well pleased" echoes Isaiah 42:1 (the Servant of the Lord). This trinitarian theophany — Father speaking, Spirit descending, Son present — reveals the identity of Jesus as the anointed Davidic King, the sacrificial beloved Son, and the obedient Servant. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The voice from heaven constitutes a divine decree of identification and commissioning before the heavenly assembly.

verses.flow: Then Jesus was led up into the wilderness by the Spirit, to be tempted by the devil.
verses.note: GREEK: "Anēchthē" (ἀνήχθη, from anagō, Strong's G321) means "was led up," indicating ascent from the Jordan valley into the higher wilderness terrain. "Peirasthēnai" (πειρασθῆναι, from peirazō, G3985) carries the dual sense of testing and temptation; the purpose clause with the infinitive shows this was divinely ordained. TYPOLOGY: Jesus recapitulates Israel's wilderness testing (Deut 8:2). Where Israel failed over forty years, the true Son of God endures forty days. The Spirit's leading parallels Israel being led through the wilderness by God (Exod 13:18).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having fasted forty days and forty nights, afterward he was hungry.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And the tempter, having come to him, said, "If you are the Son of God, command that these stones become bread."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle dative singular neuter

verses.flow: But he, answering, said, "It is written: Not on bread alone shall man live, but on every word proceeding through the mouth of God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 from the LXX, the very passage about Israel's wilderness manna provision. The "word proceeding from the mouth of God" (rhēmati ekporeuomenō dia stomatos theou) emphasizes that divine speech, not material bread, is the ultimate sustainer of life. TYPOLOGY: As the new Israel and the faithful Son, Jesus succeeds where Adam and Israel failed—trusting the Father's provision rather than seizing sustenance autonomously.

verses.flow: Then the devil takes him along into the holy city, and set him upon the pinnacle of the temple,

and says to him, "If you are the Son of God, cast yourself down, for it is written: He shall give his angels charge concerning you, and upon their hands they shall bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone."
verses.note: GREEK: The devil quotes Psalm 91:11-12 (LXX Ps 90:11-12) but omits the phrase "in all your ways," subtly distorting the promise of protection within obedience into a presumptuous demand for spectacle. THEOLOGICAL: Satan employs Scripture with precision yet decontextualizes it—a hermeneutical warning. The "pinnacle" (pterygion, G4419, literally "little wing") likely refers to the southeastern corner of the temple complex overlooking the Kidron Valley.

verses.flow: Jesus said to him, "Again it is written: You shall not put to the test the Lord your God."
verses.note: GREEK: "Ekpeiraseis" (ἐκπειράσεις, from ekpeirazō, G1598) is an intensified form of peirazō, meaning "to put to the thorough test" or "to test utterly." Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:16, which references Israel's testing of God at Massah (Exod 17:1-7). TYPOLOGY: Israel tested the Lord at Massah by demanding proof of God's presence; Jesus refuses to replicate that faithless demand, demonstrating perfect trust in the Father.

verses.flow: Again the devil takes him along to a very high mountain, and shows him all the kingdoms of the world and the glory of them,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and said to him, "All these things I will give to you, if, falling down, you will worship me."
Then Jesus says to him, "Depart, Satan! For it is written: The Lord your God you shall worship, and him alone you shall serve."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 6:13 (LXX), the foundational Shema-context command of exclusive worship. "Latreuseis" (λατρεύσεις, G3000) denotes cultic service rendered only to God. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Satan's offer to give "all the kingdoms" presupposes his delegated authority over the nations (cf. Luke 4:6; Dan 10:13, 20). In divine council theology, the nations were allotted to lesser spiritual beings (Deut 32:8-9 LXX), but Jesus reclaims all authority through obedience, not compromise with the rebel.

verses.flow: Then the devil leaves him, and behold, angels came and were ministering to him.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The angelic ministry to Jesus after the devil's departure echoes the divine council's service to the faithful King. Angels serve the Son who refused to command them for his own comfort (v.6). TYPOLOGY: This scene reverses Eden—where Adam succumbed and was expelled from the garden with its provision, Jesus overcomes in the barren wilderness and receives heavenly sustenance (cf. 1 Kgs 19:5-7, Elijah in the wilderness).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Now having heard that John had been delivered up, he withdrew into Galilee.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having left Nazareth, he came and dwelt in Capernaum, which is by the sea, in the regions of Zebulun and Naphtali,

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular neuter
verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: so that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled, saying:
"Land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali, the way of the sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the nations—
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Matthew quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 (LXX Isa 8:23-9:1), a prophecy originally concerning the Assyrian conquest of the northern tribes. The very territories first to fall into darkness under judgment are the first to see the messianic light. "Galilee of the nations" (Galilaía tōn ethnōn) underscores the Gentile-mixed character of the region, foreshadowing the universal scope of Christ's kingdom. HISTORICAL: Zebulun and Naphtali were the tribal territories devastated by Tiglath-Pileser III in 732 BC (2 Kgs 15:29).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: the people sitting in darkness saw a great light, and to those sitting in the region and shadow of death, a light has dawned upon them."
From that time Jesus began to proclaim and to say, "Repent, for the kingdom of the heavens has drawn near."
verses.note: GREEK: "Metanoeite" (μετανοεῖτε, from metanoeō, G3340) means "change your mind/thinking"—a radical reorientation of one's entire disposition toward God, not merely emotional regret. "Ēngiken" (ἤγγικεν, perfect tense of engizō, G1448) indicates the kingdom "has drawn near" and remains in a state of nearness—an already-inaugurated reality. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' proclamation is identical to John the Baptist's (Matt 3:2), yet now the King himself announces his own kingdom. The "kingdom of the heavens" (basileia tōn ouranōn) is Matthew's characteristic phrase, equivalent to "kingdom of God," using "heavens" in Jewish reverential idiom.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And walking beside the Sea of Galilee, he saw two brothers, Simon, the one called Peter, and Andrew his brother, casting a net into the sea, for they were fishermen.
And he says to them, "Come after me, and I will make you fishers of men."
verses.note: GREEK: "Halieis anthrōpōn" (ἁλιεῖς ἀνθρώπων)—"fishers of men"—uses halieus (G231), a term for professional fishermen. TYPOLOGY: The fishing imagery echoes Jeremiah 16:16 and Ezekiel 47:10, where God sends fishermen to gather his people. The call transforms their vocation into an eschatological mission of gathering people into the kingdom.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they, immediately leaving the nets, followed him.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: And going on from there, he saw another two brothers, James the son of Zebedee and John his brother, in the boat with Zebedee their father, mending their nets, and he called them.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And they, immediately leaving the boat and their father, followed him.

verses.flow: And he went about in all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues and proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom and healing every disease and every affliction among the people.
verses.note: GREEK: Three present participles—"teaching" (didaskōn), "proclaiming" (kēryssōn), and "healing" (therapeuōn)—describe the continuous threefold nature of Jesus' Galilean ministry. "Euangelion tēs basileias" (εὐαγγέλιον τῆς βασιλείας, "gospel of the kingdom") denotes the royal announcement that God's sovereign reign is being inaugurated through Jesus. HISTORICAL: Synagogues (synagōgai, G4864) served as local assemblies for Torah reading and instruction; Jesus' teaching in them indicates his authoritative role as an itinerant rabbi who proclaimed far more than Torah exposition.

verses.flow: And the report of him went out into all Syria, and they brought to him all who were ill, afflicted with various diseases and torments, those possessed by demons, and those who were moonstruck, and paralytics—and he healed them.
verses.note: GREEK: "Daimonizomenous" (δαιμονιζομένους, G1139) refers to those under the direct influence or possession of demons (daimonia). "Selēniazomenous" (σεληνιαζομένους, G4583) literally means "moonstruck," referring to epileptic-like symptoms anciently associated with lunar cycles. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The catalogue of afflictions—demonic possession, seizures, paralysis—represents the full spectrum of spiritual and physical bondage from which Christ liberates. His authority over demons demonstrates his supremacy over the hostile spiritual powers aligned against humanity (cf. Col 2:15).

verses.flow: And great crowds followed him from Galilee and the Decapolis and Jerusalem and Judea and beyond the Jordan.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And seeing the crowds, he went up onto the mountain, and when he had sat down, his disciples came to him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The phrase "went up onto the mountain" (anebē eis to oros) deliberately echoes Moses ascending Sinai (Ex 19:3). Jesus sits to teach, the authoritative rabbinic posture (kathisantos autou), presenting himself as the definitive Torah-teacher. TYPOLOGY: As Moses delivered the Law from the mountain, so Christ delivers the fulfilled Law from a mountain, establishing the new-Moses typology that pervades Matthew's Gospel (Deut 18:15).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having opened his mouth, he was teaching them, saying:
Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "Makarioi" (μακάριοι, Strong's G3107) denotes not merely "happy" but a state of divine blessing and favor; in the LXX it translates Hebrew 'ashre (Ps 1:1). "Ptōchoi" (πτωχοί, G4434) means utterly destitute, not merely humble — those reduced to begging. "Tō pneumati" (dative of reference) specifies the domain: destitute with respect to spirit, acknowledging complete spiritual poverty before God. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "kingdom of the heavens" (basileia tōn ouranōn) is unique to Matthew, reflecting Jewish reverence in circumlocuting the divine name; it is equivalent to Mark's "kingdom of God."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted.
Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This beatitude directly quotes Psalm 37:11 (LXX 36:11): "the meek shall inherit the earth" (klēronomēsousin tēn gēn). "Praeis" (πραεῖς, G4239) denotes gentle strength under control, not weakness. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus applies the Psalm's eschatological land-promise to his followers, extending the Abrahamic inheritance beyond the land of Israel to the whole renewed earth (Rom 4:13).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied.
Blessed are the merciful, for they shall receive mercy.
Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God.
Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God.
verses.note: GREEK: "Eirēnopoioi" (εἰρηνοποιοί, G1518) is a compound noun appearing only here in the NT — those who actively make peace, not merely peaceful persons. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "sons of God" (huioi theou) carries immense theological weight. In the OT, "sons of God" (bene elohim) designates divine council members (Job 1:6; Ps 82:6). Jesus promises that peacemakers will share in this exalted filial status, reflecting Paul's theology of adoption (Rom 8:14-19).

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed are those who have been persecuted on account of righteousness, for theirs is the kingdom of the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dedioigmenoi" (δεδιωγμένοι) is a perfect passive participle — those who have been and remain in a state of persecution. The perfect tense indicates an ongoing condition, not a single event. STRUCTURE: This eighth beatitude forms an inclusio with v.3, as both conclude with "for theirs is the kingdom of the heavens," framing the entire Beatitudes section.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Blessed are you when they revile you and persecute you and speak every evil thing against you, lying, on account of me.
Rejoice and be exceedingly glad, for great is your reward in the heavens; for so they persecuted the prophets who were before you.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: You are the salt of the earth; but if the salt becomes tasteless, with what shall it be salted? It is good for nothing any longer, except to be cast out and trampled underfoot by men.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: You are the light of the world. A city set upon a mountain cannot be hidden.
Neither do they light a lamp and place it under the basket, but upon the lampstand, and it gives light to all who are in the house.
So let your light shine before men, that they may see your good works and glorify your Father who is in the heavens.
Do not think that I came to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I did not come to abolish, but to fulfill.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Katalysai" (καταλῦσαι, G2647) means to dismantle, dissolve, or destroy utterly — used of tearing down buildings. "Plērōsai" (πληρῶσαι, G4137) means to fill up to the full, to bring to completion. Jesus does not abrogate Torah but brings it to its intended fullness and eschatological goal. TYPOLOGY: "The Law and the Prophets" encompasses the entire Hebrew Scripture; Christ is the telos (end/goal) toward which all Scripture pointed (Rom 10:4; Luke 24:27).

verses.flow: For truly I say to you, until heaven and earth pass away, not one iota or one stroke shall pass away from the Law, until all things come to pass.
verses.note: GREEK: "Iōta" (ἰῶτα) is the smallest letter in the Hebrew alphabet (yod); "keraia" (κεραία, G2762) refers to the tiny serif or decorative stroke distinguishing similar Hebrew letters. Together they express the absolute permanence of even the minutest detail of Scripture. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus claims an authority over Torah interpretation that exceeds any rabbi or prophet — he speaks as the one who authored it.

verses.flow: Whoever therefore loosens one of the least of these commandments and teaches men so, shall be called least in the kingdom of the heavens; but whoever does and teaches them, this one shall be called great in the kingdom of the heavens.
For I say to you, that unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you shall by no means enter into the kingdom of the heavens.
You have heard that it was said to those of old, "You shall not murder," and whoever murders shall be liable to the judgment.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I say to you that everyone who is angry with his brother shall be liable to the judgment; and whoever says to his brother, "Raka!" shall be liable to the council; and whoever says, "Fool!" shall be liable to the Gehenna of fire.
verses.note: GREEK: "Rhaka" (Ῥακά) is an Aramaic term of contempt (reqa) meaning "empty-headed" or "worthless." "Mōre" (Μωρέ) from Greek mōros (G3474) means "fool" but carries connotations of moral rebellion and godlessness (cf. Ps 14:1 LXX, "the fool said in his heart"). HISTORICAL: The "synedrion" (συνέδριον, G4892) refers to the Sanhedrin, the Jewish high court. Jesus escalates from local judgment to the supreme court to Gehenna, showing that inner anger carries the same gravity as outward murder. THEOLOGICAL: "Gehenna" (γέεννα, G1067) derives from the Valley of Hinnom (Ge-Hinnom) south of Jerusalem, where child sacrifice occurred (2 Kgs 23:10; Jer 7:31), becoming the image of final eschatological judgment.

verses.flow: If therefore you bring your gift to the altar, and there remember that your brother has something against you,

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: leave your gift there before the altar, and go; first be reconciled to your brother, and then, having come, offer your gift.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Be well-disposed toward your adversary quickly, while you are with him on the way, lest the adversary deliver you to the judge, and the judge to the officer, and you be cast into prison.
Truly I say to you, you shall by no means come out from there until you have paid the last kodrantes.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kodrantēs" (κοδράντης, G2835) is a Latin loanword (quadrans), the smallest Roman coin worth 1/64 of a denarius. This detail reflects the Roman economic context of first-century Palestine and emphasizes completeness of payment.

verses.flow: You have heard that it was said, "You shall not commit adultery."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I say to you that everyone who looks at a woman in order to desire her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.
And if your right eye causes you to stumble, tear it out and cast it from you; for it is better for you that one of your members should perish, and not your whole body be cast into Gehenna.
And if your right hand causes you to stumble, cut it off and cast it from you; for it is better for you that one of your members should perish, and not your whole body go away into Gehenna.
And it was said, "Whoever divorces his wife, let him give her a certificate of divorce."

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb perfect passive participle accusative singular feminine

verses.flow: But I say to you that everyone who divorces his wife, except on account of sexual immorality, makes her to commit adultery; and whoever marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
Again, you have heard that it was said to those of old, "You shall not swear falsely, but shall render to the Lord your oaths."
But I say to you, do not swear at all: neither by heaven, for it is the throne of God;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Isaiah 66:1 (LXX): "Heaven is my throne, and the earth is the footstool of my feet." By forbidding oaths by heaven, earth, and Jerusalem, he asserts that all creation belongs to God and cannot serve as a lesser guarantee. THEOLOGICAL: The prohibition against swearing reflects the holiness of God's name and the expectation that the people of the kingdom speak with such integrity that oaths become unnecessary.

verses.flow: nor by the earth, for it is the footstool of his feet; nor toward Jerusalem, for it is the city of the great King.
Neither shall you swear by your head, for you are not able to make one hair white or black.
But let your word be "Yes, yes; no, no"; and what is beyond these is from the evil one.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ek tou ponērou" (ἐκ τοῦ πονηροῦ) can be translated "from the evil one" (masculine, referring to Satan) or "from evil" (neuter, referring to the condition). The same ambiguity appears in the Lord's Prayer (Matt 6:13). THEOLOGICAL: Speech beyond simple truthfulness originates in the fallen order; kingdom citizens are called to such radical truthfulness that oath-taking is rendered superfluous.

verses.flow: You have heard that it was said, "An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth."
But I say to you, do not resist the evil one; but whoever strikes you on your right cheek, turn to him also the other.
verses.note: GREEK: "Rhapizei" (ῥαπίζει, G4474) means to strike with the back of the hand, a gesture of insult and social humiliation rather than a blow intended to injure. Striking the right cheek with the right hand implies a backhanded slap. HISTORICAL: In Jewish and Roman culture, a backhanded slap was an insult delivered by a superior to an inferior. Turning the other cheek thus forces the aggressor to acknowledge the victim as an equal.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: And to the one wishing to sue you and to take your tunic, let him have also the cloak.
And whoever compels you to go one mile, go with him two.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine
verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Give to the one who asks of you, and do not turn away from the one wishing to borrow from you.
You have heard that it was said, "You shall love your neighbor and hate your enemy."

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But I say to you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command "love your enemies" (agapate tous echthrous) represents the radical ethic of the kingdom. "Agapate" (ἀγαπᾶτε, G25) is a present imperative — a continuous, willed disposition, not a feeling. TYPOLOGY: This command finds its supreme fulfillment in Christ himself, who prayed for his executioners (Luke 23:34) and died for enemies (Rom 5:10).

verses.flow: so that you may become sons of your Father who is in the heavens, for he causes his sun to rise upon the evil and the good, and sends rain upon the righteous and the unrighteous.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Sons of your Father" (huioi tou patros hymōn) echoes the divine council sonship motif. Believers who reflect God's indiscriminate mercy participate in the divine family. THEOLOGICAL: God's common grace — sun and rain given to evil and good alike — becomes the model for kingdom ethics. The ground of the command is imitation of the Father, not reciprocity.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: For if you love those who love you, what reward do you have? Do not even the tax collectors do the same?
And if you greet your brothers only, what do you do that is extraordinary? Do not even the Gentiles do the same?
You therefore shall be perfect, as your heavenly Father is perfect.
verses.note: GREEK: "Teleioi" (τέλειοι, G5046) means complete, mature, having reached the intended end (telos) — not sinless perfection but wholeness of character. THEOLOGICAL: This verse summarizes the entire Sermon's ethic: kingdom citizens are called to reflect the complete character of God himself. TYPOLOGY: Echoes Leviticus 19:2 ("Be holy, for I the Lord your God am holy") and Deuteronomy 18:13 ("You shall be blameless before the Lord your God"), reframing the holiness code through the lens of the Father's perfection revealed in Christ.

verses.flow: But take heed not to do your righteousness before men so as to be seen by them; otherwise you have no reward with your Father who is in the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: Προσέχετε (prosechete, Strong's G4337) means "take heed" or "give attention to," carrying a sense of careful vigilance. Δικαιοσύνην (dikaiosynēn, G1343) here refers broadly to righteous deeds or acts of piety, encompassing the alms, prayer, and fasting that follow. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as the thesis statement for the entire section (6:1-18), which is structured in three parallel panels: almsgiving (vv.2-4), prayer (vv.5-15), and fasting (vv.16-18), each with the same pattern of warning against hypocrisy and promise of the Father's reward.

verses.flow: Therefore when you do alms, do not sound a trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, so that they may be glorified by men. Truly I say to you, they have received their reward in full.
verses.note: GREEK: Σαλπίσῃς (salpisēs, G4537) "sound a trumpet" is likely a vivid metaphor for ostentatious self-announcement rather than a literal practice. Ὑποκριταί (hypokritai, G5273) originally denoted stage actors in Greek theater who wore masks; Jesus repurposes the term for those performing piety as a theatrical display. HISTORICAL: The synagogues and streets (ῥύμαις, narrow lanes) represent the most public venues of Jewish communal life in first-century Palestine.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: But when you do alms, let not your left hand know what your right hand does,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: so that your alms may be in secret; and your Father who sees in secret shall repay you.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And when you pray, you shall not be as the hypocrites, for they love to stand praying in the synagogues and on the corners of the broad streets, so that they may appear to men. Truly I say to you, they have received their reward in full.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The repetition of the hypocrite formula establishes that prayer, like almsgiving, can be corrupted by the desire for human approval. The phrase ἀπέχουσι τὸν μισθόν ("they have received their reward in full") uses a commercial term (ἀπέχω, G568) meaning "paid in full" — a receipt term found on papyri, indicating that human praise is the only payment they will ever receive.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But you, when you pray, enter into your inner room and having shut your door, pray to your Father who is in secret; and your Father who sees in secret shall repay you.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And when praying, do not use vain repetitions as the Gentiles do, for they suppose that in their many words they shall be heard.
verses.note: GREEK: Βατταλογήσητε (battalogēsēte, G945) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, possibly onomatopoeic ("batta-batta"), meaning to babble or use meaningless repetitions. Πολυλογία (polylogia, G4180) is also a NT hapax, meaning "much speaking" or "wordiness." THEOLOGICAL: Jesus contrasts pagan prayer, which assumes gods must be persuaded through quantity of words, with prayer to a Father who already knows His children's needs (v.8).

verses.flow: Therefore do not be made like them, for your Father knows what things you have need of before you ask Him.
Therefore pray thus, you: Our Father, who is in the heavens, let Your name be hallowed;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Lord's Prayer begins with Πάτερ ἡμῶν ("Our Father"), establishing the covenantal relationship between God and His people as the ground of all petition. The first petition, ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου ("let Your name be hallowed"), is an aorist passive imperative — a "divine passive" requesting that God Himself act to sanctify His own name among the nations. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Ezekiel 36:23 (LXX) where God declares He will sanctify His great name among the nations (cf. Isa 29:23).

verses.flow: let Your kingdom come; let Your will be done, as in heaven, so also upon earth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Ἐλθέτω ἡ βασιλεία σου ("let Your kingdom come") is the central eschatological petition, asking for the full manifestation of God's sovereign reign inaugurated in Christ's ministry (Matt 4:17). The parallel clause "as in heaven, so also upon earth" envisions the divine council's perfect order in the heavenly realm being replicated on earth. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The heavenly realm where God's will is already perfectly enacted by the angelic host serves as the template for earthly reality — the petition asks that the rebellion of earth be brought under the same order as heaven.

verses.flow: Give us this day our daily bread;
verses.note: GREEK: Ἐπιούσιον (epiousios, G1967) is one of the rarest words in Greek literature, virtually a hapax legomenon outside this passage and its parallel in Luke 11:3. Its meaning is debated: it may derive from ἐπί + οὐσία ("for existence/substance," i.e., bread necessary for sustenance) or from ἐπί + ἰοῦσα (ἡμέρα) ("for the coming day"). TYPOLOGY: The petition echoes the daily manna provision in the wilderness (Exod 16:4), casting the disciples as the new Israel sustained by the Father during their sojourn.

verses.flow: and forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors;
and lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Πειρασμόν (peirasmon, G3986) can mean either "temptation" or "testing/trial." The petition does not imply God tempts (cf. Jas 1:13) but asks the Father not to bring His children into a situation of severe testing. Τοῦ πονηροῦ (tou ponērou) is ambiguous in Greek — either "evil" (neuter, abstract) or "the evil one" (masculine, personal). Given Matthew's usage elsewhere (Matt 13:19, 38), the masculine reading designating Satan is likely intended. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Israel's wilderness testing and anticipates Jesus' own testing by the devil (Matt 4:1-11).

verses.flow: For if you forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.
But if you do not forgive men, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And when you fast, do not become as the hypocrites, of a gloomy countenance; for they disfigure their faces so that they may appear to men to be fasting. Truly I say to you, they have received their reward in full.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But you, when fasting, anoint your head and wash your face,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: so that you may not appear to men to be fasting, but to your Father who is in secret; and your Father who sees in secret shall repay you.
Do not store up for yourselves treasures upon the earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break through and steal;
verses.note: GREEK: Βρῶσις (brōsis, G1035) literally means "eating" or "corrosion" — likely referring to rust, vermin, or general decay rather than a specific agent. Διορύσσουσιν (dioryssousin, G1358) means "dig through," reflecting the mud-brick construction of first-century Palestinian houses, which thieves could literally tunnel through. HISTORICAL: Wealth in the ancient world was stored as physical goods — garments, grain, precious metals — all vulnerable to the agents Jesus names.

verses.flow: but store up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust destroys, and where thieves do not break through nor steal.
For where your treasure is, there will be your heart also.
The lamp of the body is the eye. If therefore your eye be single, your whole body shall be full of light.
verses.note: GREEK: Ἁπλοῦς (haplous, G573) means "single" or "sound," carrying connotations of generosity and wholeness in Jewish moral discourse. A "single" eye denotes undivided loyalty and generous perception, while a πονηρός ("evil") eye (v.23) in Semitic idiom specifically connotes stinginess and envy (cf. Deut 15:9; Prov 28:22). THEOLOGICAL: The eye metaphor bridges the teachings on treasure (vv.19-21) and on serving two masters (v.24), showing that one's inner disposition determines whether the whole person walks in light or darkness.

verses.flow: But if your eye be evil, your whole body shall be dark. If therefore the light that is in you is darkness, how great is the darkness!
No one is able to serve two lords; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will hold to the one and despise the other. You are not able to serve God and Mammon.
verses.note: GREEK: Μαμωνᾷ (mamōna) is a transliteration of the Aramaic māmōnā, meaning "wealth" or "property." It is here personified as a rival lord demanding the allegiance that belongs to God alone. THEOLOGICAL: The absolute statement οὐδεὶς δύναται ("no one is able") establishes the impossibility of divided ultimate allegiance. The two verbs for rejection — μισέω ("hate") and καταφρονέω ("despise/look down upon") — and two for loyalty — ἀγαπάω ("love") and ἀντέχομαι ("hold to/cling to") — present totalizing categories that admit no middle ground. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The language of "serving lords" echoes the covenant demand for exclusive worship of YHWH over against all rival spiritual powers (Deut 6:13-14).

verses.flow: Because of this I say to you, do not be anxious for your life, what you shall eat, nor for your body, what you shall put on. Is not the life more than food, and the body more than clothing?
Look at the birds of the heaven, that they do not sow, nor reap, nor gather into barns; and your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not of much more value than they?

verses.flow: And which of you by being anxious is able to add one cubit to his span of life?
And why are you anxious about clothing? Observe the lilies of the field, how they grow: they do not toil nor do they spin.
But I say to you that not even Solomon in all his glory was arrayed as one of these.

verb present passive participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: But if God so clothes the grass of the field, which exists today and tomorrow is cast into the oven, will He not much more clothe you, O you of little faith?
verses.note: GREEK: Ὀλιγόπιστοι (oligopistoi, G3640) — "O you of little faith" — is a distinctively Matthean term (cf. Matt 8:26; 14:31; 16:8), a compound of ὀλίγος ("little") and πίστις ("faith"). It is not a rebuke of unbelief but of insufficient trust in the Father's providential care. THEOLOGICAL: The argument moves from lesser to greater (qal wahomer): if God adorns the transient grass with glory surpassing Solomon's, how much more will He provide for those made in His image and called to His kingdom.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore do not be anxious, saying, "What shall we eat?" or "What shall we drink?" or "What shall we wear?"
For all these things the Gentiles eagerly seek; for your heavenly Father knows that you have need of all these things.
But seek first the kingdom and His righteousness, and all these things shall be added to you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is the climactic imperative of the entire section on anxiety (vv.25-34). Ζητεῖτε πρῶτον ("seek first") establishes a priority of allegiance, not a sequence of tasks — the kingdom and its righteousness must be the orienting pursuit of life. TYPOLOGY: "His righteousness" (τὴν δικαιοσύνην αὐτοῦ) recalls the δικαιοσύνη of v.1, forming an inclusio for the chapter: true righteousness is not performed before men but is the gift and demand of the kingdom of the heavenly Father. The promise that "all these things shall be added" echoes the covenant blessings of Deuteronomy 28:1-14 for those who walk in faithfulness.

verses.flow: Therefore do not be anxious for tomorrow, for tomorrow will be anxious for itself. Sufficient for the day is its own evil.
Do not judge, so that you be not judged.
verses.note: GREEK: κρίνετε (krinete, G2919) is present active imperative of krinō, meaning "to judge, decide, distinguish." The prohibition with μή (mē) + present imperative suggests ceasing an ongoing action. This is not a prohibition against all moral discernment (cf. v.16, v.20) but against a censorious, self-righteous spirit of condemnation. THEOLOGICAL: The passive κριθῆτε ("you be judged") is a divine passive — God is the implied agent of final judgment.

verses.flow: For with what judgment you judge, you shall be judged; and with what measure you measure, it shall be measured to you.
verses.note: GREEK: κρίματι (krimati, G2917) refers to the verdict or sentence rendered, while μέτρῳ (metrō, G3358) denotes a standard of measurement. The parallelism is Hebraic in structure, reflecting Jesus's thoroughly Jewish rhetorical style. THEOLOGICAL: The principle of reciprocal divine judgment echoes wisdom tradition (Prov 11:17; cf. Matt 6:14-15).

verses.flow: And why do you look at the speck that is in the eye of your brother, but the beam that is in your own eye you do not perceive?
Or how will you say to your brother, "Permit me, I will cast out the speck from your eye," and behold, the beam is in your own eye?
Hypocrite, cast out first the beam from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to cast out the speck from the eye of your brother.
verses.note: GREEK: ὑποκριτά (hypokrita, G5273) derives from Greek theater — one who acts a part behind a mask. Jesus appropriates this term to describe religious pretenders. THEOLOGICAL: The call to self-examination before correction of others establishes priority of personal holiness, not elimination of all fraternal correction (cf. Gal 6:1).

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Do not give what is holy to the dogs, nor cast your pearls before the swine, lest they trample them under their feet and, turning, tear you to pieces.
verses.note: GREEK: τὸ ἅγιον (to hagion, "the holy thing") and μαργαρίτας (margaritas, G3135, "pearls") represent sacred truths or kingdom realities. THEOLOGICAL: This verse balances the prohibition of judgmentalism in vv.1-5 — discernment of persons is still required. The dogs and swine were ritually unclean animals in Jewish law (Lev 11:7, 27), here representing those hostile to the holy. HISTORICAL: In Second Temple Judaism, swine symbolized Gentile impurity and Roman oppression.

verses.flow: Ask, and it shall be given to you; seek, and you shall find; knock, and it shall be opened to you.
verses.note: GREEK: Three present active imperatives — αἰτεῖτε (aiteite, "ask"), ζητεῖτε (zēteite, "seek"), κρούετε (krouete, "knock") — form an escalating triad of persistence in prayer. The present tense implies continuous action: "keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking." STRUCTURE: The three imperatives each receive a corresponding promise in the passive/future, emphasizing God's certain response.

verses.flow: If therefore you, being evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in the heavens give good things to those who ask Him?
verses.note: GREEK: πονηροί (ponēroi, G4190) — "evil" — is a strong term Jesus applies to fallen humanity. πόσῳ μᾶλλον (posō mallon) is the qal wahomer argument (lesser to greater), a standard rabbinic hermeneutical method. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between evil human fathers and the heavenly Father establishes God's surpassing goodness. The Father "in the heavens" (ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς) situates God in the divine council space from which He governs and dispenses gifts.

verses.flow: Therefore all things, whatever you desire that men should do to you, so also you do to them; for this is the Law and the Prophets.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This "Golden Rule" is presented as the summation of the Law and the Prophets (ὁ νόμος καὶ οἱ προφῆται), paralleling Jesus's statement in Matt 22:37-40. Unlike the negative formulations in Hillel (b. Shabbat 31a) and Tobit 4:15, Jesus states it positively, demanding active love. TYPOLOGY: Jesus here presents Himself as the authoritative interpreter of the entire Mosaic covenant, fulfilling His claim in Matt 5:17.

verses.note: GREEK: τεθλιμμένη (tethlimmenē, G2346) is a perfect passive participle of thlibō, meaning "pressed together, afflicted, compressed." It implies the way has been made narrow by external pressure — tribulation. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the two ways echoes Deut 30:15-19 and the "Two Ways" tradition found in Second Temple literature (Didache 1-6, 1QS 3:13-4:26). Life (ζωήν) here denotes eschatological life in the age to come.

verses.flow: Not everyone who says to Me, "Lord, Lord," shall enter into the kingdom of the heavens, but the one who does the will of My Father who is in the heavens.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is among the most christologically significant statements in the Sermon. Jesus claims that entrance into the kingdom of the heavens depends on relationship to Him personally — "says to Me" (μοι), "My Father" (τοῦ πατρός μου). He positions Himself as eschatological Judge who grants or denies entry to God's kingdom. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus assumes the role of the divine King who administers the heavenly βασιλείαν (basileia), executing the will of the Father as co-regent over the divine realm.

verses.flow: Many will say to Me on that day, "Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in Your name, and in Your name cast out demons, and in Your name perform many mighty works?"

verses.flow: And then I will declare to them, "Never did I know you; depart from Me, you who work lawlessness."
verses.note: GREEK: ὁμολογήσω (homologēsō, G3670) means "I will declare openly, confess publicly." οὐδέποτε ἔγνων ὑμᾶς (oudepote egnōn hymas) — "never did I know you" — uses the aorist of ginōskō, implying not merely intellectual awareness but covenantal relational knowledge (cf. Amos 3:2, Jer 1:5). ἀνομίαν (anomian, G458) means "lawlessness," echoing Ps 6:8 (LXX). TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Ps 6:9 (LXX 6:8), placing Himself in the position of the psalmist-king who pronounces judgment — yet here He surpasses David as the final Judge.

verses.flow: Therefore everyone who hears these words of Mine and does them shall be likened to a wise man, who built his house upon the rock.
verses.note: GREEK: ὁμοιωθήσεται (homoiōthēsetai, G3666) — future passive, "shall be likened." φρονίμῳ (phronimō, G5429) denotes practical wisdom, prudence — not mere knowledge but applied understanding. πέτραν (petran, G4073) is bedrock, massive stone. TYPOLOGY: The image of building on rock evokes the sure foundation of Isa 28:16 and the rock imagery associated with God throughout the OT (Deut 32:4, 2 Sam 22:2, Ps 18:2). Jesus identifies His own words as the foundation equivalent to the word of God.

verses.flow: And the rain came down, and the rivers came, and the winds blew and fell upon that house, and it did not fall, for it had been founded upon the rock.

And the rain came down, and the rivers came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell — and great was the fall of it.
And it came to pass, when Jesus finished these words, the crowds were astonished at His teaching,

verses.flow: for He was teaching them as one having authority, and not as their scribes.
verses.note: GREEK: ἐξουσίαν (exousian, G1849) means "authority, right, power derived from one's position." Unlike the scribes (γραμματεῖς, grammateis, G1122) who cited chains of rabbinic tradition, Jesus taught as one possessing inherent divine authority. HISTORICAL: Scribal teaching in Second Temple Judaism operated by appeal to prior authorities — "Rabbi X said in the name of Rabbi Y." Jesus's direct "But I say to you" (Matt 5:22, 28, 32, 34, 39, 44) was unprecedented and implied divine prerogative.

verses.flow: And behold, a leper, having come forward, was worshiping Him, saying, "Lord, if You are willing, You are able to cleanse me."
verses.note: GREEK: "Prosekynei" (προσεκύνει, Strong's G4352) denotes an act of prostrate worship or obeisance, imperfect tense indicating repeated or ongoing action. The leper's address "Kyrie" (Lord) combined with worship suggests recognition of divine authority. His confession "if You are willing, You are able" reveals faith in Christ's power while submitting to His sovereign will.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And having stretched out the hand, He touched him, saying, "I am willing; be cleansed." And immediately his leprosy was cleansed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus touching the leper is profoundly significant. Under Levitical law (Lev 13-14), contact with a leper rendered one ceremonially unclean. Yet instead of Jesus becoming unclean, the uncleanness departs — the flow of holiness reverses the flow of impurity. The authoritative "I am willing" (Thelō) mirrors the divine fiat of creation.

verses.flow: And Jesus says to him, "See that you tell no one, but go, show yourself to the priest, and offer the gift which Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The instruction to show oneself to the priest and offer the gift "which Moses commanded" (Lev 14:1-32) confirms Jesus' fulfillment of Torah rather than its abolition (Matt 5:17). The phrase "eis martyrion autois" (for a testimony to them) implies the cleansing itself serves as evidence of messianic authority to the priestly establishment.

verses.flow: And He says to him, "I, having come, will heal him."
verses.note: GREEK: "Egō elthōn therapeusō auton" — the emphatic pronoun "Egō" (I Myself) placed first is striking. Some scholars read this as a question: "Shall I, having come, heal him?" — emphasizing the remarkable nature of a Jewish teacher entering a Gentile's home. The word "therapeusō" (G2323) denotes both healing and attentive service.

verses.flow: And when Jesus heard this, He marveled and said to those following, "Truly I say to you, with no one in Israel have I found such great faith.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus "marveling" (ethaumasen, G2296) is remarkable — the divine Son expressing genuine astonishment at a Gentile centurion's faith, greater than anything found in Israel. This foreshadows the inclusion of the nations into the covenant people and anticipates the Great Commission (Matt 28:19).

verses.flow: And I say to you that many will come from the east and the west and will recline at table with Abraham and Isaac and Jacob in the kingdom of the heavens,
but the sons of the kingdom will be cast out into the outer darkness; there will be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Sons of the kingdom" (huioi tēs basileias) denotes those who by covenant heritage belonged to God's kingdom — ethnic Israel. Their casting into "outer darkness" (to skotos to exōteron) represents eschatological exclusion from the messianic banquet. The "weeping and gnashing of teeth" is a fixed formula in Matthew for final judgment (Matt 13:42, 22:13, 24:51, 25:30).

verses.flow: And Jesus said to the centurion, "Go; as you have believed, let it be done to you." And the servant was healed in that hour.

verses.flow: so that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled, saying, "He Himself took our infirmities and bore our diseases."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This quotation from Isaiah 53:4 (LXX) is applied by Matthew to Jesus' healing ministry, identifying Him as the Suffering Servant. The Hebrew of Isa 53:4 uses "nasa" (bore/carried) and "sabal" (carried as a burden), which the LXX renders differently. Matthew's Greek "elaben" (took) and "ebastasen" (bore) emphasizes physical healing as an inaugurated aspect of the Servant's atoning work.

verses.flow: And a certain scribe, having come forward, said to Him, "Teacher, I will follow You wherever You go."
And Jesus says to him, "The foxes have dens, and the birds of the heaven have nests, but the Son of Man has not where He may lay the head."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Son of Man" (ho huios tou anthrōpou) is Jesus' primary self-designation, evoking Daniel 7:13-14 where the "one like a son of man" receives universal dominion from the Ancient of Days. The irony is profound: the One to whom all authority belongs has nowhere to lay His head in His own creation. GREEK: "Kataskenōseis" (G2682) — "nesting places," used only here and in Luke 9:58.

But Jesus says to him, "Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead."

verses.flow: And when He had entered into the boat, His disciples followed Him.

verses.note: GREEK: "Seismos megas" (σεισμὸς μέγας) — literally "a great shaking," the same word used for earthquakes (G4578). Matthew deliberately uses this cosmic-scale term rather than Mark's "lailaps" (storm), suggesting a supernatural disturbance. TYPOLOGY: Jesus sleeping in the storm-tossed vessel echoes Jonah 1:5, a typological connection Jesus Himself draws in Matt 12:40.

In ancient Near Eastern cosmology, the sea represented chaos and hostile spiritual forces. Christ exercises the authority of the Creator God over the primal elements — the disciples' question in v27 rightly perceives the theophanic significance.

verses.flow: And behold, they were bringing to him a paralytic, laid upon a bed. And Jesus, seeing their faith, said to the paralytic, "Take courage, child; your sins are forgiven."
verses.note: GREEK: "Aphientai" (ἀφίενται, from ἀφίημι, Strong's G863) is a present passive indicative — "are being forgiven" — indicating a present divine action, not merely a future promise. "Paralytikos" (παραλυτικός, G3885) denotes one loosened or disabled in the limbs. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus addresses the man's sins before his body, revealing that the deeper human crisis is spiritual. The plural "their faith" (τὴν πίστιν αὐτῶν) includes both the paralytic and those carrying him.

verses.flow: And behold, some of the scribes said within themselves, "This man blasphemes."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The scribes' charge of blasphemy rests on the theological axiom that only God can forgive sins (Isa 43:25; Ps 51:4). Their reasoning is technically correct — which is precisely why Jesus' claim constitutes an implicit assertion of divine prerogative. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus acts with the authority of the divine throne, exercising a function reserved for YHWH alone in Second Temple Jewish understanding.

verses.flow: Then the disciples of John come to him, saying, "Why do we and the Pharisees fast often, but your disciples do not fast?"

And Jesus said to them, "Are the sons of the bridechamber able to mourn as long as the bridegroom is with them? But days will come when the bridegroom is taken away from them, and then they will fast."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "bridegroom" (νυμφίος, G3566) is a messianic and divine title — YHWH is portrayed as Israel's bridegroom throughout the prophets (Isa 62:5; Hos 2:16-20; Jer 2:2). THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "taken away" (ἀπαρθῇ, from ἀπαίρω, G522) foreshadows the violent removal of the Messiah — his crucifixion — anticipating the passion narrative.

verses.flow: And seeing the crowds, he was moved with compassion concerning them, because they were harassed and cast down, as sheep not having a shepherd.
verses.note: GREEK: "Esplanchnisthē" (ἐσπλαγχνίσθη, G4697) derives from "splanchna" (σπλάγχνα, bowels/innards) — a visceral, gut-level compassion unique in the Gospels to Jesus and to characters representing God in parables. "Eskylmenoi" (ἐσκυλμένοι, from σκύλλω, G4660) means "flayed" or "mangled," and "errimmenoi" (ἐρριμμένοι, from ῥίπτω, G4496) means "thrown down" or "cast aside." TYPOLOGY: The shepherd-sheep imagery echoes Ezekiel 34:5-6, where YHWH indicts Israel's failed shepherds and promises to shepherd his flock himself — Jesus fulfills this divine role.

verses.flow: Then he says to his disciples, "The harvest indeed is plentiful, but the workers are few."
"Therefore, pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest, that he may send out workers into his harvest."
verses.note: GREEK: "Deēthēte" (δεήθητε, from δέομαι, G1189) is an aorist passive imperative — an urgent command to petition. "Ekbalē" (ἐκβάλῃ, from ἐκβάλλω, G1544) means "thrust out" or "cast forth" — the same forceful verb used for casting out demons, suggesting the urgency and divine compulsion behind the sending of workers. THEOLOGICAL: The "Lord of the harvest" (τοῦ κυρίου τοῦ θερισμοῦ) is God himself; Jesus instructs his disciples to pray to the Father, yet in Matthew 10:1-5 Jesus himself sends the workers — a subtle convergence of divine authority.

verses.flow: And having summoned His twelve disciples, He gave them authority over unclean spirits, so as to cast them out, and to heal every disease and every infirmity.
verses.note: GREEK: "Exousian" (ἐξουσίαν, G1849) denotes delegated authority, not merely power (dynamis). Jesus grants His own divine authority to the Twelve over the spirit realm. "Pneumatōn akathartōn" (unclean spirits) reflects Second Temple Jewish understanding of hostile spiritual beings operating under demonic hierarchy. THEOLOGICAL: This commissioning establishes the apostolic office as an extension of Christ's own messianic authority over the powers of darkness.

verses.flow: Now of the twelve apostles the names are these: first, Simon, the one called Peter, and Andrew his brother; James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother;
verses.note: GREEK: "Apostolos" (ἀπόστολος, G652) means "one sent forth" — a commissioned emissary with the authority of the sender. This is the first use of the term in Matthew. "Prōtos" (first) applied to Simon Peter indicates not merely sequential listing but a position of primacy among the Twelve. HISTORICAL: The number twelve deliberately mirrors the twelve tribes of Israel, signaling the reconstitution of God's people under the Messiah.

verses.flow: Philip and Bartholomew; Thomas and Matthew the tax collector; James the son of Alphaeus, and Thaddaeus;

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The restriction to Israel ("road of the Gentiles" and "city of the Samaritans") reflects the salvation-historical priority of the covenant people (Rom 1:16). This limitation is temporary, reversed in the Great Commission (Matt 28:19). HISTORICAL: "Samaritans" were a mixed population with heterodox worship at Mount Gerizim, considered outside the covenant community by mainstream Judaism.

verses.note: GREEK: "Ēngiken" (ἤγγικεν, G1448) is a perfect active indicative of engizō — "has drawn near" with abiding results. The kingdom is not merely approaching but has arrived in proximity through the presence and mission of the King. THEOLOGICAL: This proclamation mirrors Jesus's own opening message (Matt 4:17) and John the Baptist's (Matt 3:2), establishing continuity in the prophetic announcement of God's reign breaking into the present age.

verses.flow: And whoever does not receive you nor hear your words, going out of that house or city, shake off the dust of your feet.

verses.note: GREEK: "Anektoteron" (ἀνεκτότερον) is a comparative form meaning "more bearable/tolerable" — a hapax in the Gospels outside its parallel usage. TYPOLOGY: Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19) serve as the paradigmatic type of divine judgment against wickedness. Jesus declares that rejecting the apostolic witness of the kingdom surpasses even Sodom's guilt, since greater revelation brings greater accountability (cf. Heb 10:29).

verses.flow: Behold, I send you as sheep in the midst of wolves; therefore be wise as the serpents and innocent as the doves.
verses.note: GREEK: "Phronimoi" (φρόνιμοι, G5429) denotes practical wisdom or shrewdness, not mere intelligence. "Akeraioi" (ἀκέραιοι, G185) means unmixed, pure, without alloy — used of metals or wine without adulteration. TYPOLOGY: The serpent imagery echoes Genesis 3 (the shrewd nachash), while the dove recalls Noah's dove (Gen 8:8-12) and the Spirit's descent at Jesus's baptism (Matt 3:16). Disciples must combine discernment with purity.

verses.flow: But beware of men, for they will deliver you over to councils, and in their synagogues they will scourge you.

verses.flow: For it is not you who are speaking, but the Spirit of your Father who speaks in you.

This is an early articulation of the Spirit's role in empowering witness under trial. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The Father's Spirit operating through human agents reflects the divine council pattern where God's purposes are mediated through chosen representatives in the earthly realm.

verses.flow: And brother will deliver up brother to death, and a father his child; and children will rise up against parents and put them to death.
