verses.flow: And you will be hated by all on account of My name. But the one having endured to the end, this one shall be saved.
And when they persecute you in this city, flee to the other; for truly I say to you, you shall by no means finish the cities of Israel before the Son of Man comes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The Son of Man comes" (ἔλθῃ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου) draws on Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man receives dominion from the Ancient of Days. The statement's fulfillment is debated — it may refer to the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70 as a vindication-coming, or to the ongoing mission that extends until the Parousia. GREEK: "Ou mē telesēte" is an emphatic double negative with the aorist subjunctive — "you shall by no means finish."

verses.flow: A disciple is not above the teacher, nor a servant above his lord.
It is sufficient for the disciple that he become as his teacher, and the servant as his lord. If they called the master of the house Beelzebul, how much more the members of his household!
verses.note: GREEK: "Beelzeboul" (Βεελζεβούλ) likely derives from Hebrew ba'al zebul, "lord of the dwelling/height," a deliberate corruption of the Philistine deity Baal-Zebub, "lord of the flies" (2 Kgs 1:2). DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus identifies the accusation as attributing His work to the prince of demons — a direct challenge to the spiritual warfare framework. If the Master of the house is slandered as serving the enemy council, His household members should expect no less.

verses.flow: And do not fear those who kill the body but are not able to kill the soul; but rather fear the One who is able to destroy both soul and body in Gehenna.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between killing the body and destroying both soul and body in Gehenna establishes the reality of eschatological judgment beyond physical death. "The One who is able" (τὸν δυνάμενον) refers to God alone as sovereign judge. GREEK: "Gehenna" (γέεννα, G1067) derives from the Valley of Hinnom (gê-hinnom) south of Jerusalem, associated with child sacrifice (2 Chr 28:3; Jer 7:31) and later with the eschatological place of fiery judgment in Second Temple literature (1 Enoch 27:1-2).

verses.flow: But even the hairs of your head are all numbered.
Therefore do not fear; you are of more value than many sparrows.
Everyone therefore who shall confess Me before men, I also will confess him before My Father who is in the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "Homologēsō" (ὁμολογήσω, G3670) means "I will confess/acknowledge" — literally "to say the same thing," implying public agreement and identification. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus claims the prerogative to speak as advocate before the Father in the heavenly court. The "before My Father who is in the heavens" language evokes the divine council assembly scene where the Son of Man serves as intercessor or witness for His faithful ones (cf. Dan 7:13-14; Rev 3:5).

verses.flow: But whoever denies Me before men, I also will deny him before My Father who is in the heavens.
Do not think that I came to cast peace upon the earth; I came not to cast peace, but a sword.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The sword (μάχαιραν, G3162) is not a military weapon here but a metaphor for division. Jesus subverts messianic expectations of immediate political peace, echoing Micah 7:6 in the following verses. His coming as Messiah necessarily divides those who receive the kingdom from those who reject it. THEOLOGICAL: True shalom comes only through the eschatological work of Christ; the present age is marked by the sword of division as allegiance to the King supersedes all other loyalties.

verses.flow: For I came to set a man against his father, and a daughter against her mother, and a bride against her mother-in-law;

verses.flow: The one having found his soul shall lose it, and the one having lost his soul on account of Me shall find it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Psychē" (ψυχή, G5590) here carries the full range of meaning: life, soul, self. It should not be reduced to merely "life" in the modern sense. THEOLOGICAL: This paradox of losing to find stands at the heart of discipleship theology. The one who clings to self-preservation at the cost of faithfulness to Christ loses his true existence, while the one who surrenders his psychē for Christ's sake discovers it in the resurrection. This saying appears in all four Gospels (Mark 8:35; Luke 9:24; John 12:25).

verses.flow: The one who receives you receives Me, and the one who receives Me receives the One who sent Me.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chain of reception — receiving the apostle equals receiving Christ equals receiving the Father — establishes the shaliach principle: the sent one bears the full authority of the sender. HISTORICAL: In Jewish law, "a man's agent is as the man himself" (Mishnah Berakhot 5:5). Jesus applies this legal concept to the apostolic mission, grounding their authority in His own, which is grounded in the Father's. This verse forms the theological basis for apostolic authority in the early church.

verses.flow: Now John, having heard in the prison the works of the Christ, sent word through his disciples
verses.note: GREEK: "τὰ ἔργα τοῦ χριστοῦ" (ta erga tou Christou) — "the works of the Christ." The definite article before Christos (G5547) is significant: Matthew identifies Jesus not merely as an anointed figure but as THE Christ, the promised Anointed One. John hears of these works while in Herod Antipas's prison at Machaerus. HISTORICAL: John was imprisoned by Herod Antipas for condemning his marriage to Herodias (Matt 14:3-4). His question from prison reflects not unbelief but the tension between messianic expectation of a conquering deliverer and Jesus's ministry of healing and proclamation.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and said to him, "Are you the Coming One, or shall we expect another?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "ὁ ἐρχόμενος" (ho erchomenos) — "the Coming One" is a recognized messianic title drawn from Ps 118:26 and Mal 3:1. The participle with the article functions as a proper title. John's question whether to "expect another" (ἕτερον, heteron — another of a different kind, G2087) implies he wonders whether Jesus is the final messianic figure or a forerunner of yet another.

verses.flow: And Jesus, answering, said to them, "Go and report to John the things which you hear and see:
the blind receive sight and the lame walk, lepers are cleansed and the deaf hear, and the dead are raised and the poor have good tidings proclaimed to them.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus's response is a composite allusion to Isaiah 35:5-6, 29:18-19, 61:1, and 26:19. Rather than asserting his messianic identity directly, he points to the fulfillment of prophetic signs. The inclusion of "the dead are raised" (νεκροὶ ἐγείρονται) goes beyond Isaiah's explicit language and signals the in-breaking of eschatological power. The poor receiving good tidings (πτωχοὶ εὐαγγελίζονται) echoes Isa 61:1 (LXX), the programmatic text Jesus read in Luke 4:18-19.

verses.flow: And blessed is the one who is not caused to stumble on account of me."
verses.note: GREEK: "σκανδαλισθῇ" (skandalisthē, G4624) — from skandalon, a trap-stick or stumbling block. The passive voice suggests being "caused to stumble" or "entrapped" rather than merely offended. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus pronounces a blessing on those who do not find his unexpected messianic profile — suffering servant rather than conquering king — to be a cause of falling away. This is a gentle exhortation directed at John himself.

verses.flow: But what did you go out to see? A man clothed in soft garments? Behold, those wearing soft garments are in the houses of kings.
But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and one more excellent than a prophet.
This is he concerning whom it has been written: "Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who shall prepare your way before you."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: A conflation of Mal 3:1 and Exod 23:20 (LXX). In Malachi, God says "I send my messenger before MY face," but Jesus changes the pronoun to "YOUR face," placing himself in the position of the Lord (YHWH) before whom the messenger prepares the way. This is a profound christological claim: Jesus identifies himself as the Lord of Malachi's prophecy and John as the promised messenger-angel (ἄγγελος, angelos, G32).

verses.flow: Truly I say to you, among those born of women there has not arisen one greater than John the Baptist; yet the least in the kingdom of the heavens is greater than he.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox is striking: John is the greatest figure in all of redemptive history prior to the kingdom's inauguration, yet "the least in the kingdom of the heavens" surpasses him. This does not diminish John but exalts the new covenant reality. GREEK: "μικρότερος" (mikroteros, G3398) is comparative — "lesser" or "least" — and likely refers to rank or position within the inaugurated kingdom rather than moral or personal stature.

verses.flow: And from the days of John the Baptist until now, the kingdom of the heavens suffers violence, and violent men seize it by force.
verses.note: GREEK: "βιάζεται" (biazetai, G971) can be either middle voice ("forcefully advances") or passive ("suffers violence"). "βιασταί" (biastai, G973) is a hapax legomenon in the NT — "violent ones" who "seize" (ἁρπάζουσιν, harpazousin) the kingdom. THEOLOGICAL: The interpretation is debated: either (1) violent opponents (Herod, religious leaders) attack the kingdom and its agents, or (2) the spiritually forceful press into it with holy urgency. The context of John's imprisonment favors a negative sense of violent opposition.

verses.flow: For all the prophets and the Law prophesied until John.

verses.flow: And if you are willing to receive it, he himself is Elijah, the one who is about to come.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies John the Baptist with the Elijah figure prophesied in Mal 4:5 (LXX 3:23) who would come "before the great and terrible day of the Lord." The conditional "if you are willing to receive it" acknowledges the difficulty of this identification, since John did not literally restore all things as expected. John comes in the spirit and power of Elijah (Luke 1:17), fulfilling the typological pattern.

verses.flow: The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and they say, "Behold, a gluttonous man and a wine-drinker, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!" And wisdom was vindicated by her works.
verses.note: GREEK: "ἐδικαιώθη ἡ σοφία ἀπὸ τῶν ἔργων αὐτῆς" — "wisdom was vindicated by her works." The verb δικαιόω (dikaioō, G1344) here means "proven right" or "vindicated." THEOLOGICAL: Personified Wisdom (σοφία, sophia) likely refers to God's wise plan enacted through both John's asceticism and Jesus's fellowship with sinners. Both are vindicated not by the approval of this generation but by the fruit (works) they produce. This echoes the Wisdom tradition of Proverbs 8 and Sirach.

verses.flow: Then he began to reproach the cities in which most of his mighty works had been done, because they did not repent:

verses.flow: "Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works which were done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes.
But I say to you, it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon in the day of judgment than for you.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative plural feminine

verses.flow: And you, Capernaum, shall you be exalted to heaven? You shall be brought down to Hades. For if the mighty works which were done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The language of exaltation and descent echoes Isa 14:13-15 (LXX), originally directed against the king of Babylon but carrying overtones of cosmic rebellion. Capernaum, Jesus's own base of ministry (Matt 4:13), receives the severest warning. "Hades" (ᾅδης, hadēs, G86) here denotes the realm of the dead and judgment, contrasted with the heavenly exaltation Capernaum presumed for itself. HISTORICAL: Sodom was the paradigmatic city of judgment in Jewish tradition (Gen 19; Ezek 16:48-50), making this comparison devastatingly severe.

verses.flow: But I say to you that it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment than for you."

verses.flow: At that time Jesus, answering, said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you hid these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to infants.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus addresses the Father as "Lord of heaven and earth" (κύριε τοῦ οὐρανοῦ καὶ τῆς γῆς), affirming God's absolute sovereignty over all realms — both the heavenly council and the earthly domain. GREEK: "ἐξομολογοῦμαί" (exomologoumai, G1843) means "I praise" or "I confess openly" — a term of worship. The divine reversal of hiding truth from the "wise and understanding" (σοφῶν καὶ συνετῶν) and revealing it to "infants" (νηπίοις, nēpiois) echoes the pattern of 1 Cor 1:19-27.

verses.flow: Yes, Father, for thus it was well-pleasing before you.

All things have been delivered to me by my Father, and no one fully knows the Son except the Father, nor does anyone fully know the Father except the Son, and the one to whom the Son wills to reveal him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is sometimes called "the Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the theology of John's Gospel. "ἐπιγινώσκει" (epiginōskei, G1921) denotes full, intimate knowledge — not mere awareness but relational knowing. The mutual exclusive knowledge between Father and Son asserts the unique divine identity of Jesus: only God can fully know God. The Son's sovereign will (βούληται, boulētai) to reveal the Father places him as the sole mediator of divine knowledge. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The exclusive mutual knowledge between Father and Son sets the Son apart from all other heavenly beings; no angel or member of the divine council possesses this reciprocal knowledge of the Father.

verses.flow: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Δεῦτε πρός με" (Deute pros me) — "Come to me" — is a divine invitation echoing the call of personified Wisdom in Prov 9:5 and Sir 24:19, and ultimately YHWH's own invitation in Isa 55:1-3. Jesus speaks with divine authority, offering rest (ἀναπαύσω, anapausō) that in the OT only God can grant. GREEK: "κοπιῶντες" (kopiontes, G2872) denotes exhausting toil, and "πεφορτισμένοι" (pephortismenoi, G5412) is a perfect passive participle — those who have been loaded down and remain burdened, likely referring to the heavy yoke of Pharisaic legal interpretation.

verses.flow: Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you shall find rest for your souls.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Rest for your souls" (ἀνάπαυσιν ταῖς ψυχαῖς ὑμῶν) is a direct quotation from Jer 6:16 (LXX), where God calls Israel to walk in the ancient paths and find rest. Jesus applies YHWH's own promise to himself. GREEK: "πραΰς" (praus, G4239) — "gentle" or "meek" — echoes the LXX of Zech 9:9, the messianic king who comes "meek, riding on a donkey." The "yoke" (ζυγός, zygos, G2218) was a common rabbinic metaphor for Torah-obedience; Jesus offers his own yoke — his teaching and lordship — as the true fulfillment of the Law.

verses.flow: For my yoke is easy and my burden is light."
In that time Jesus went through the grainfields on the Sabbath; and His disciples were hungry and began to pluck heads of grain and to eat.

verses.flow: But the Pharisees, seeing this, said to Him, "Behold, your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on a Sabbath."
But He said to them, "Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, and those with him—

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular neuter

verses.flow: how he entered into the house of God, and they ate the bread of the Presentation, which was not lawful for him to eat, nor for those with him, but for the priests alone?"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus references 1 Samuel 21:1-6 where David ate the bread of the Presentation (τοὺς ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως, tous artous tēs protheseōs), also called the "showbread." David as the anointed-but-not-yet-enthroned king prefigures Christ's own authority over temple regulations. The argument is from lesser to greater: if David could override ceremonial law in necessity, how much more the Son of David.

verses.flow: "Or have you not read in the Law that on the Sabbaths the priests in the temple profane the Sabbath and are blameless?"
"But I say to you that something greater than the temple is here."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The neuter comparative μεῖζον (meizon, "something greater") rather than masculine is striking—Jesus does not simply say "someone greater" but points to the entire reality of God's kingdom presence surpassing the temple institution. This is a radical claim: the locus of God's dwelling has shifted from the temple to the person and mission of Jesus (John 2:19-21).

verses.flow: "But if you had known what this is: 'I desire mercy and not sacrifice,' you would not have condemned the blameless."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus quotes Hosea 6:6 (LXX) for the second time in Matthew (cf. Matt 9:13). The prophetic priority of ἔλεος (eleos, "mercy," "covenant faithfulness," translating Hebrew ḥesed) over θυσία (thysia, "sacrifice") reframes the entire Sabbath dispute: God's heart is for loyal love, not mere ritual compliance.

verses.flow: "For the Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath."
verses.note: GREEK: κύριος (kyrios, "Lord," Strong's G2962) here applied to the Son of Man asserts sovereign authority over the Sabbath itself. THEOLOGICAL: Since God instituted the Sabbath (Gen 2:2-3), claiming lordship over it is an implicit claim to divine prerogative. The title "Son of Man" evokes Daniel 7:13-14, where the one like a son of man receives dominion over all things.

verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct quotation of Isaiah 42:1-4, the first Servant Song. The Greek παῖς (pais, "servant/child," Strong's G3816) translates Hebrew ʿebed. Matthew identifies Jesus as the Isaianic Servant par excellence. verses.flow: But knowing their thoughts, He said to them, "Every kingdom divided against itself is laid waste, and every city or house divided against itself shall not stand.
"And if Satan casts out Satan, he is divided against himself. How then shall his kingdom stand?"
"And if I by Beelzebul cast out the demons, by whom do your sons cast them out? Therefore they shall be your judges."
"But if by the Spirit of God I cast out the demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the clearest statements of inaugurated eschatology in the Gospels. ἔφθασεν (ephthasen, aorist of φθάνω, "has come upon," Strong's G5348) indicates the kingdom of God has already arrived, not merely approached. DIVINE_COUNCIL: If Jesus casts out demons by the Spirit of God (ἐν πνεύματι θεοῦ), this demonstrates that God's sovereign authority is actively reclaiming territory from hostile spiritual powers—a divine council conflict motif.

verses.flow: "Or how is anyone able to enter into the house of the strong man and plunder his goods, unless he first binds the strong man? And then he will plunder his house."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "strong man" (ὁ ἰσχυρός, ho ischyros) represents Satan, and his "house" represents his domain of spiritual oppression. Jesus is the one who binds the strong man—a motif of the divine warrior conquering the rebel powers (cf. Isa 49:24-25). The verb δήσῃ (dēsē, "bind") anticipates the ultimate binding of Satan in eschatological judgment (Rev 20:2). This parable frames Jesus' exorcisms as acts of cosmic warfare and territorial liberation.

verses.flow: "The one who is not with Me is against Me, and the one who does not gather with Me scatters."
"Therefore I say to you, every sin and blasphemy shall be forgiven men, but the blasphemy against the Spirit shall not be forgiven.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The distinction between forgivable blasphemy and the unforgivable blasphemy against the Spirit (τοῦ πνεύματος βλασφημία) is set in the context of attributing the Holy Spirit's work to demonic agency. This is not a single careless word but a settled, willful attribution of God's power to Satan—effectively calling good evil. The gravity lies in the deliberate rejection of the Spirit's testimony to Christ.

verses.flow: "And whoever speaks a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but whoever speaks against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven him, neither in this age nor in the one to come."
verses.note: GREEK: αἰῶνι (aiōni, "age," Strong's G165) refers not to "world" in the spatial sense but to an era or epoch. Jesus distinguishes "this age" (τούτῳ τῷ αἰῶνι) from "the one to come" (τῷ μέλλοντι)—a standard Jewish apocalyptic framework dividing history into the present evil age and the coming messianic age. THEOLOGICAL: The unforgiveness extends across both ages, indicating an absolute and irrevocable condition resulting from persistent rejection of the Spirit's witness.

verses.flow: Then some of the scribes and Pharisees answered Him, saying, "Teacher, we wish to see a sign from You."

verses.flow: But He answered and said to them, "An evil and adulterous generation seeks after a sign, and no sign shall be given to it except the sign of Jonah the prophet.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "sign of Jonah" is the premier typological foreshadowing of Christ's death and resurrection. The "evil and adulterous generation" (γενεὰ πονηρὰ καὶ μοιχαλίς) uses Old Testament covenant-unfaithfulness language—Israel as the unfaithful bride (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16). Their demand for a sign while rejecting the sign-giver demonstrates spiritual adultery.

verses.flow: "For just as Jonah was in the belly of the great sea creature three days and three nights, so shall the Son of Man be in the heart of the earth three days and three nights."
verses.note: GREEK: κήτους (kētous, "great sea creature," Strong's G2785) follows the LXX of Jonah 2:1 (κήτει μεγάλῳ). TYPOLOGY: The "three days and three nights" parallel establishes Jonah's entombment in the sea creature as a type of Christ's burial in the earth. The "heart of the earth" (τῇ καρδίᾳ τῆς γῆς) parallels "the belly of the sea creature" (τῇ κοιλίᾳ τοῦ κήτους), connecting descent and resurrection as a single redemptive event (cf. Eph 4:9-10).

verses.flow: "Men of Nineveh shall rise up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it, because they repented at the preaching of Jonah; and behold, something greater than Jonah is here."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Ninevites' repentance at Jonah's preaching stands as a rebuke to Jesus' contemporaries. The neuter πλεῖον (pleion, "something greater") again points beyond Jesus' person to the entire reality of the kingdom He inaugurates. Gentile pagans responded to a reluctant prophet; how much more culpable is the generation that rejects the Messiah Himself.

verses.flow: "The Queen of the South shall be raised up in the judgment with this generation and shall condemn it, because she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and behold, something greater than Solomon is here."

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The parable of the returning unclean spirit warns against mere moral reformation without genuine spiritual transformation. HISTORICAL: In the context of Second Temple Judaism, this may address a generation that had external religious reform but rejected the messianic Spirit's filling.

verses.flow: And stretching out His hand toward His disciples, He said, "Behold, My mother and My brothers!"

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus redefines kinship around obedience to the Father's will (τὸ θέλημα τοῦ πατρός μου), establishing the new covenant community. Biological descent gives way to spiritual relationship—a radical redefinition that anticipates the inclusion of Gentiles into the family of God. "My Father who is in the heavens" (τοῦ πατρός μου τοῦ ἐν οὐρανοῖς) reinforces Jesus' unique filial relationship with God while extending familial identity to all who do the Father's will.

verses.flow: And in them is being fulfilled the prophecy of Isaiah, the one saying: "By hearing you shall hear and shall by no means understand, and seeing you shall see and shall by no means perceive;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct citation of Isaiah 6:9-10 (LXX), the commissioning passage where God tells Isaiah that his preaching will harden Israel. Jesus identifies his own ministry as fulfilling the same pattern of prophetic proclamation met with judicial hardening. The quotation formula ἀναπληροῦται ("is being fulfilled") uses a compound form suggesting a progressive, cumulative fulfillment (cf. Isa 6:9-10).

verses.flow: for the heart of this people has grown dull, and with their ears they heard heavily, and their eyes they have closed, lest they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears and understand with their heart, and should turn back, and I shall heal them."
verses.note: GREEK: ἐπαχύνθη (epachynthē, G3975) — "has grown fat/thick/dull," from παχύνω. The LXX follows the Hebrew הַשְׁמֵן, depicting a heart made insensible, calloused beyond receptivity. ἐκάμμυσαν (ekammysan, G2576) — "they have closed" (their eyes), a rare verb appearing only here in the NT, emphasizing willful refusal to see. THEOLOGICAL: The passage presents both divine sovereignty in judicial hardening and human culpability in willful blindness — a tension maintained throughout Scripture (cf. Rom 9:18; 11:7-8).

verses.flow: But blessed are your eyes, because they see, and your ears, because they hear.
For truly I say to you that many prophets and righteous ones desired to see what you see, and did not see it, and to hear what you hear, and did not hear it.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Many prophets and righteous ones — a reference to the long line of OT saints who longed to see the messianic age (cf. 1 Pet 1:10-12; Heb 11:13, 39-40). Second Temple Judaism was marked by intense expectation of God's coming kingdom. Jesus declares that what the disciples witness is the very eschatological reality the prophets yearned for.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: ἄτιμος (atimos, G820) — "without honor, dishonored." verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus' mighty works are conditioned not by his capacity but by the recipients' faith. ἀπιστίαν (apistian, G570) — "unbelief" — functions here not merely as intellectual doubt but as a settled refusal to trust. verses.flow: And he, answering, said, "It is not good to take the bread of the children and to cast it to the little dogs."

verses.flow: And she said, "Yes, Lord; for even the little dogs eat from the crumbs falling from the table of their masters."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The woman's reply is one of the most remarkable displays of faith in the Gospels. She does not dispute the salvation-historical priority of Israel ("the children") but argues from within Jesus' own metaphor: even the κυνάρια (kynaria, G2952, diminutive "little dogs," household pets, not wild scavengers) partake of overflow from the master's table. She affirms that the messianic blessing is so abundant it overflows Israel's boundaries. TYPOLOGY: This anticipates the eschatological ingathering of the nations promised in Isaiah 49:6 and Isaiah 56:6-8.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Then Jesus, answering, said to her, "O woman, great is your faith! Let it be done to you as you desire." And her daughter was healed from that hour.
verses.note: GREEK: Ὦ γύναι (ō gynai) — the interjection Ὦ expresses deep emotion, here admiration. μεγάλη σου ἡ πίστις (megalē sou hē pistis) — "great is your faith" — stands in sharp contrast to the disciples' repeated "little faith" (ὀλιγοπιστία, Matt 6:30; 8:26; 14:31). THEOLOGICAL: The immediate healing at a distance, effected by Jesus' sovereign word, demonstrates his divine authority over the spiritual realm. A Gentile woman's faith surpasses that of Israel — a theme that anticipates the Great Commission (Matt 28:19).

The two feeding miracles together (twelve baskets for Israel, seven for the nations) depict the fullness of God's provision for all peoples through Christ.

HISTORICAL: The Pharisees and Sadducees were theologically opposed factions rarely united; their joint approach underscores the extraordinary nature of their opposition to Jesus.

THEOLOGICAL: "Adulterous" (moichalis, G3428) echoes the OT prophetic tradition of Israel's covenant unfaithfulness as spiritual adultery (Hos 2:2; Jer 3:8-9).

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But Jesus, knowing this, said, "Why do you reason among yourselves, O you of little faith, because you have no bread?
Do you not yet understand, nor remember the five loaves of the five thousand, and how many baskets you took up?
Nor the seven loaves of the four thousand, and how many large baskets you took up?
How do you not understand that it was not concerning bread I spoke to you? But beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees."
Then they understood that he did not say to beware of the leaven of bread, but of the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "leaven" (zýmē, G2219) of the Pharisees and Sadducees is identified as their "teaching" (didachē, G1322), not their bread. Leaven as a metaphor for corrupting influence permeates both OT and NT usage (1 Cor 5:6-8; Gal 5:9).

verses.flow: And Jesus, having come into the region of Caesarea Philippi, was asking his disciples, saying, "Who do men say the Son of Man to be?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Caesarea Philippi was a pagan cultic center at the base of Mount Hermon, associated with the worship of Pan and with divine council traditions in Second Temple Judaism regarding the fallen watchers (1 Enoch 6:6). Jesus' question about his identity at this location is theologically loaded. GREEK: "Son of Man" (ho huios tou anthrōpou) evokes Daniel 7:13-14, the divine figure who receives an everlasting kingdom.

verses.flow: And they said, "Some, John the Baptist; and others, Elijah; and others, Jeremiah or one of the prophets."
He says to them, "But you, who do you say that I am?"

verses.flow: And Simon Peter, answering, said, "You are the Christ, the Son of the living God."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's confession — "You are the Christ, the Son of the living God" — is the climactic christological declaration of Matthew's Gospel. "The Christ" (ho Christos) = the Anointed One, the long-awaited Davidic King (2 Sam 7:12-14; Ps 2:2, 7). "The living God" (tou theou tou zōntos) distinguishes YHWH from the dead idols worshipped at Caesarea Philippi.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus, answering, said to him, "Blessed are you, Simon Bar-Jonah, for flesh and blood did not reveal this to you, but my Father who is in the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "Bar-Jonah" (Βαριωνᾶ) is Aramaic patronymic, "son of Jonah." THEOLOGICAL: Jesus affirms that Peter's confession was divinely revealed — "flesh and blood did not reveal this to you" — indicating that true knowledge of Christ's identity comes by the Father's sovereign disclosure (cf. Matt 11:27; 1 Cor 12:3).

verses.flow: And I also say to you that you are Peter, and upon this rock I will build my assembly, and the gates of Hades shall not prevail against it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Petros" (Πέτρος, G4074) is masculine = a stone or rock-piece; "petra" (πέτρα, G4073) is feminine = bedrock or massive rock. The wordplay is deliberate. THEOLOGICAL: "Gates of Hades" (pylai hadou) is a cosmic-warfare image — Hades as the realm of the dead cannot withstand or overpower Christ's assembly. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In Second Temple thought, the "gates of the underworld" represented the stronghold of death and hostile spiritual powers; Christ's ekklēsia is the reconstituted people of God that storms this domain through resurrection power.

verses.flow: I will give to you the keys of the kingdom of the heavens, and whatever you bind upon the earth shall be having been bound in the heavens, and whatever you loose upon the earth shall be having been loosed in the heavens."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "keys of the kingdom" signify delegated authority under Christ the King (cf. Isa 22:22, the key of the house of David). GREEK: The periphrastic future perfects "shall be having been bound" (estai dedemenon) and "shall be having been loosed" (estai lelymenon) indicate that what is bound or loosed on earth will have already been bound or loosed in heaven — earthly apostolic authority ratifies heavenly decisions.

verses.flow: Then he strictly charged the disciples that they should tell no one that he is the Christ.

From that time Jesus began to show to his disciples that it is necessary for him to go away to Jerusalem and to suffer many things from the elders and chief priests and scribes, and to be killed, and on the third day to be raised.

verses.flow: And Peter, having taken him aside, began to rebuke him, saying, "May God be merciful to you, Lord! This shall never be to you."

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But he, having turned, said to Peter, "Get behind me, Satan! You are a stumbling block to me, for you do not set your mind on the things of God, but the things of men."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Jesus addresses Peter as "Satan" (Σατανᾶ, G4567) — the Adversary — because Peter's rebuke functioned as a satanic temptation to avoid the cross, echoing the wilderness temptation (Matt 4:8-10). GREEK: "Skandalon" (σκάνδαλον, G4625) = a stumbling block or trap-trigger, a snare set to cause falling.

verses.flow: Then Jesus said to his disciples, "If anyone desires to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.
For whoever desires to save his soul shall lose it, and whoever loses his soul for my sake shall find it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Psychē" (ψυχή, G5590) here carries the full weight of "soul" or "life" — the totality of one's existence. The paradox of losing to find is central to the kingdom ethic of Jesus. TYPOLOGY: This pattern of death-to-life echoes the grain of wheat motif (John 12:24-25) and prefigures Christ's own death and resurrection as the paradigmatic act of gaining through losing.

verses.flow: For what shall a man be profited if he gains the whole world but forfeits his soul? Or what shall a man give as an exchange for his soul?
For the Son of Man is about to come in the glory of his Father with his angels, and then he will render to each according to his deed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Son of Man coming "in the glory of his Father with his angels" draws directly from Daniel 7:13-14 and Zechariah 14:5. Rendering "to each according to his deed" (kata tēn praxin autou) echoes Psalm 62:12 and Proverbs 24:12, affirming the final judgment authority of the Son of Man. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The angels (angeloi) accompanying the Son of Man form his heavenly retinue — the divine council members executing the decree of the King.

verses.flow: Truly I say to you that there are some of those standing here who shall not taste of death until they see the Son of Man coming in his kingdom."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This difficult saying has been interpreted as referring to the Transfiguration (Matt 17:1-8), the Resurrection, Pentecost, or the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70. The phrase "coming in his kingdom" (erchomenon en tē basileia autou) most likely refers to the visible manifestation of Christ's royal authority, partially realized at the Transfiguration and decisively at the Resurrection-Ascension. GREEK: "Geúsōntai thanátou" (taste of death) is a Semitic idiom for experiencing death (cf. Heb 2:9).

verses.flow: And after six days Jesus takes along Peter and James and John his brother, and brings them up into a high mountain by themselves.
And He was transfigured before them, and His face shone as the sun, and His garments became white as the light.
verses.note: GREEK: μετεμορφώθη (metemorphōthē, G3339) — "was transfigured," a divine passive indicating God's action. The verb metamorphoō denotes a change of essential form, not mere outward appearance. The same root appears in Rom 12:2 and 2 Cor 3:18, linking the believer's transformation to Christ's glorified nature.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: And behold, there appeared to them Moses and Elijah, conversing with Him.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Moses and Elijah represent the Law and the Prophets respectively, both of which bear witness to Christ (Luke 24:27). Both figures experienced theophanies on mountains — Moses at Sinai (Exod 34:29-35) and Elijah at Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8-13). Their appearing with Jesus confirms Him as the fulfillment of all prior revelation.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Peter, answering, said to Jesus, "Lord, it is good for us to be here. If You are willing, I will make here three tabernacles — one for You, and one for Moses, and one for Elijah."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Peter's offer to build three σκηνάς (skēnas, G4633) — "tabernacles" or "booths" — likely echoes the Feast of Tabernacles (Lev 23:42-43). By proposing three equal shelters, Peter unwittingly places Moses and Elijah on the same level as Jesus, an error corrected immediately by the divine voice in v.5.

verses.flow: While he was still speaking, behold, a bright cloud overshadowed them, and behold, a voice out of the cloud, saying, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased — hear Him!"
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The voice from the cloud echoes the heavenly enthronement language of Ps 2:7 ("This is My Son") combined with the Servant declaration of Isa 42:1 ("in whom I am well pleased") and the prophetic command of Deut 18:15 ("hear Him"). The bright cloud (nephélē phōteinḗ) recalls the Shekinah glory-cloud of the tabernacle and temple (Exod 40:34-35), marking this as a direct theophanic event. TYPOLOGY: The command "hear Him" identifies Jesus as the Prophet like Moses whom God promised to raise up (Deut 18:15-19).

verses.flow: And as they were coming down from the mountain, Jesus commanded them, saying, "Tell the vision to no one until the Son of Man is raised from the dead."
verses.note: GREEK: ὅραμα (horama, G3705) — "vision," used in the LXX for prophetic revelatory experiences (cf. Dan 10:7-8). But I say to you that Elijah has already come, and they did not recognize him, but did to him whatever they wished. Thus also the Son of Man is about to suffer at their hands."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the Elijah who was to come (Mal 4:5-6), fulfilling the prophetic expectation. The parallel between what "they did to him" (John's execution by Herod) and what the Son of Man is "about to suffer" establishes a typological pattern: the forerunner's fate prefigures the Messiah's passion.

verses.flow: Then the disciples understood that He spoke to them concerning John the Baptist.

verses.flow: And Jesus, answering, said, "O faithless and perverse generation! How long shall I be with you? How long shall I bear with you? Bring him here to Me."
verses.note: GREEK: γενεὰ ἄπιστος καὶ διεστραμμένη (genea apistos kai diestrammenē) — "faithless and perverse generation" echoes the Song of Moses in Deut 32:5 (LXX: γενεὰ σκολιὰ καὶ διεστραμμένη). Jesus speaks here with the authority of YHWH addressing covenant-breaking Israel. The dual rhetorical question "how long" parallels divine lament formulas in the prophets (Num 14:27; Isa 6:11).

And He says to them, "Because of your little faith. For truly I say to you, if you have faith as a grain of mustard seed, you will say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move, and nothing will be impossible for you."
verses.note: GREEK: ὀλιγοπιστίαν (oligopistian, G3640) — "little faith," a distinctly Matthean term (cf. Matt 6:30; 8:26; 14:31; 16:8). It denotes not absence of faith but its deficiency. The mustard seed comparison (κόκκον σινάπεως) uses the proverbially smallest seed to indicate that even a minute measure of genuine faith accesses the power of God. THEOLOGICAL: The mountain-moving imagery draws on prophetic eschatological language (Isa 40:4; Zech 14:4).

verses.flow: And when they had come to Capernaum, those who received the two-drachma tax came to Peter and said, "Does your Teacher not pay the two-drachma tax?"
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The δίδραχμα (didrachma, G1323) was a two-drachma coin equivalent to the half-shekel temple tax required annually of every Jewish male over twenty (Exod 30:13-16). This tax funded the daily sacrifices and temple maintenance. After 70 AD, Rome redirected this tax to the temple of Jupiter Capitolinus (Josephus, War 7.218).

verses.flow: He says, "Yes." And when he had come into the house, Jesus spoke first to him, saying, "What do you think, Simon? The kings of the earth — from whom do they receive tolls or tax? From their own sons, or from strangers?"

words.m: verb aorist active participle genitive singular masculine

verses.flow: And when he said, "From strangers," Jesus said to him, "Then the sons are free.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's argument establishes that as the Son of God, He is exempt from the temple tax — since kings do not tax their own sons. The term ἐλεύθεροι (eleutheroi, "free") carries royal-filial significance: Christ's divine sonship grants Him inherent freedom from obligations owed to His Father's house. Yet He voluntarily submits (v.27) so as not to cause stumbling — a model of condescension for the sake of others (cf. Phil 2:5-8).

verses.flow: But lest we cause them to stumble, go to the sea and cast a hook, and take up the first fish that comes up, and having opened its mouth, you will find a stater. Take that and give it to them for Me and for you."
verses.note: GREEK: στατῆρα (statēra, G4715) — a stater coin worth four drachmas, the exact amount needed to pay the temple tax for two persons. This is a hapax legomenon in the NT. THEOLOGICAL: The miracle demonstrates Jesus's sovereign lordship over creation — even fish and coins serve His purposes — while His willingness to pay reveals the voluntary humility of the King who needs not submit yet does so for the sake of His people.

verses.flow: In that hour the disciples came to Jesus, saying, "Who then is greatest in the kingdom of the heavens?"

verses.flow: And having called a little child to himself, he set it in the midst of them,
and said, "Truly I say to you, unless you are turned and become as the little children, you shall by no means enter into the kingdom of the heavens.
verses.note: GREEK: "στραφῆτε" (straphēte, aorist passive subjunctive of stréphō, G4762) means "be turned" — a passive implying divine action in conversion. The double negative "οὐ μή" (ou mē) is the strongest form of negation in Greek, expressing absolute impossibility. THEOLOGICAL: Entry into the kingdom requires not achievement but radical reorientation toward childlike dependence and trust.

verses.flow: Whoever therefore shall humble himself as this little child, this one is the greatest in the kingdom of the heavens.
And whoever shall receive one such little child in my name, receives me.

words.m: verb present active participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: But whoever shall cause one of these little ones who believe in me to stumble, it is profitable for him that a millstone turned by a donkey be hung around his neck, and he be sunk in the depth of the sea.
verses.note: GREEK: "σκανδαλίσῃ" (skandalisē, G4624) means to set a snare or cause to stumble into sin. "μύλος ὀνικός" (mylos onikos) is literally a "donkey-millstone" — the large upper millstone turned by a donkey, far heavier than a hand-mill stone. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus pronounces the severest possible consequence for those who lead vulnerable believers into sin, indicating that spiritual harm to the weak is among the gravest offenses in the kingdom.

verses.flow: Woe to the world because of stumbling-blocks! For it is necessary that the stumbling-blocks come, but woe to that man through whom the stumbling-block comes.

verses.flow: And if your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and cast it from you. It is better for you to enter into life maimed or lame, than having two hands or two feet to be cast into the eternal fire.

verses.flow: And if your eye causes you to stumble, pluck it out and cast it from you. It is better for you to enter into life with one eye, than having two eyes to be cast into the Gehenna of fire.
See that you do not despise one of these little ones, for I say to you that their angels in the heavens continually behold the face of my Father who is in the heavens.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Their angels in the heavens continually behold the face of my Father" presupposes a heavenly court where angelic beings serve as guardians and advocates before God's throne. The phrase "behold the face" (βλέπουσι τὸ πρόσωπον) echoes the court language of those with direct access to the king (cf. 2 Sam 14:24; Esth 1:14; Tob 12:15). This implies a hierarchy of spiritual beings with specific stewardship over believers.

verses.flow: What do you think? If a certain man should have a hundred sheep, and one of them should go astray, does he not leave the ninety-nine upon the mountains and, having gone, seek the one going astray?

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural neuter

verses.flow: And if it should come to pass that he finds it, truly I say to you that he rejoices over it more than over the ninety-nine that have not gone astray.
Thus it is not the will before your Father who is in the heavens that one of these little ones should perish.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "θέλημα ἔμπροσθεν" (thelēma emprosthen) — literally "will before" the Father — is a Semitic idiom meaning "it is not the will/pleasure in the presence of." This verse anchors the Parable of the Lost Sheep in divine intentionality: God's sovereign care extends to the least significant members of the community. TYPOLOGY: The shepherd imagery echoes Ezekiel 34 where God himself promises to seek his scattered sheep, a role now fulfilled through Christ (cf. John 10:11-16).

verses.flow: And if your brother sins against you, go and reprove him between you and him alone. If he hears you, you have gained your brother.
But if he does not hear, take with you yet one or two more, so that by the mouth of two or three witnesses every matter may be established.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "By the mouth of two or three witnesses every matter may be established" directly quotes Deuteronomy 19:15 (LXX). Jesus applies Mosaic judicial procedure to the discipline of the new covenant community, demonstrating continuity between Torah jurisprudence and ecclesial governance.

verses.flow: And if he refuses to hear them, tell it to the assembly. And if he refuses to hear even the assembly, let him be to you as the Gentile and the tax-collector.

verses.flow: Truly I say to you, whatever things you bind upon the earth shall be having been bound in heaven, and whatever things you loose upon the earth shall be having been loosed in heaven.
verses.note: GREEK: The periphrastic future perfect construction "ἔσται δεδεμένα" (estai dedemena) and "ἔσται λελυμένα" (estai lelymena) is significant: it literally reads "shall be having been bound/loosed," suggesting that heaven's decree precedes or ratifies the earthly action. verses.flow: And his lord, being wrathful, delivered him to the torturers until he should repay all that was owed.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The parable's conclusion is sobering: forgiveness received must produce forgiveness extended. "From your hearts" (ἀπὸ τῶν καρδιῶν) demands not merely external compliance but genuine interior transformation. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus does not abolish tithing but subordinates it to justice (κρίσις), mercy (ἔλεος), and faithfulness (πίστις) — echoing Mic 6:8 and Hos 6:6. The triad represents the moral core of Torah that the prophets consistently emphasized.

verses.flow: Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you are like whitewashed tombs, which outwardly indeed appear beautiful, but inwardly are full of dead men's bones and all uncleanness.
So also you outwardly indeed appear righteous to men, but inwardly you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness.
Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you build the tombs of the prophets and adorn the monuments of the righteous,
and you say, "If we had been in the days of our fathers, we would not have been partakers with them in the blood of the prophets."

verses.flow: So then you testify against yourselves that you are sons of those who murdered the prophets.
And you — fill up the measure of your fathers!
Serpents! Offspring of vipers! How shall you escape from the judgment of Gehenna?
verses.note: GREEK: γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν (gennēmata echidnōn) — "offspring of vipers" — echoes John the Baptist's identical indictment (Matt 3:7). TYPOLOGY: The serpent imagery deliberately connects to Gen 3:15 and the seed of the serpent. Jesus identifies these leaders not merely as failed teachers but as spiritual descendants in the line of the adversary, reinforcing the cosmic warfare motif underlying the entire discourse.

verses.flow: Therefore, behold, I send to you prophets and wise men and scribes; some of them you will kill and crucify, and some of them you will scourge in your synagogues and persecute from city to city,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus speaks with divine prerogative: ἐγὼ ἀποστέλλω — "I send" — placing himself in the role of God who sends the prophets (cf. Jer 7:25; 2 Chr 36:15-16). This is a stunning christological claim, as in the OT it is YHWH alone who commissions prophets. TYPOLOGY: The mention of crucifixion alongside scourging in synagogues anticipates both Jesus's own death and the persecution of the early church (Acts 5:40; 2 Cor 11:24-25).

verses.flow: so that upon you may come all the righteous blood poured out upon the earth, from the blood of Abel the righteous unto the blood of Zechariah son of Barachiah, whom you murdered between the temple and the altar.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Abel (Gen 4:8) is the first murder victim in the Hebrew canon; Zechariah son of Barachiah likely refers to the prophet Zechariah (Zech 1:1) or to Zechariah son of Jehoiada (2 Chr 24:20-22), whose murder in the temple court is the last recorded martyrdom in the Hebrew canonical order. TYPOLOGY: Jesus encompasses the entirety of salvation history's righteous bloodshed, presenting himself as the one upon whom all such debts converge — the ultimate righteous sufferer whose blood speaks a better word than Abel's (Heb 12:24).

verses.flow: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets and stones those sent to her — how often I desired to gather your children, in the way a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and you were not willing!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus's lament over Jerusalem reveals the heart of God grieving over his covenant people (cf. Hos 11:8; Deut 32:11). The hen-and-chicks imagery (ὄρνις, ornis) echoes the divine wing-shelter motif of Ps 17:8; 36:7; 91:4, placing Jesus in the role of YHWH as the sheltering presence of Israel. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The phrase ποσάκις ἠθέλησα — "how often I desired" — implies a history of repeated attempts to gather Israel that extends beyond Jesus's earthly ministry, suggesting his pre-incarnate divine activity.

verses.flow: Behold, your house is left to you desolate.

verses.flow: For I say to you, you shall not see me from now on, until you say, "Blessed is the One who comes in the name of the Lord."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The quotation is from Ps 118:26 (LXX 117:26), part of the Hallel psalms sung at Passover and Tabernacles. εὐλογημένος ὁ ἐρχόμενος ἐν ὀνόματι κυρίου — "Blessed is the One who comes in the name of the Lord" — was shouted at the Triumphal Entry (Matt 21:9) and will be confessed at the Parousia. THEOLOGICAL: Jesus conditions his visible return upon Israel's national confession, linking eschatology to the restoration of Israel (cf. Rom 11:25-26; Acts 3:19-21). The "house left desolate" (v. 38) echoes Jer 22:5 and anticipates the temple's destruction in AD 70.

verses.flow: And having gone out, Jesus departed from the temple, and his disciples came to him to show him the buildings of the temple.

verses.flow: And he, answering, said to them, "Do you not see all these things? Truly I say to you, there shall not be left here stone upon stone which shall not be thrown down."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The temple buildings, expanded massively by Herod the Great beginning ca. 20 BC, were renowned throughout the ancient world for their magnificence. Jesus' prophecy was fulfilled in AD 70 when Titus and the Roman legions destroyed the temple, literally leaving no stone upon stone. The Greek katalythēsetai (Strong's G2647) means "to utterly demolish, dismantle."

verses.flow: And as he was sitting upon the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately, saying, "Tell us, when shall these things be, and what is the sign of your coming and of the consummation of the age?"
verses.note: GREEK: The disciples ask three interconnected questions. The word parousia (G3952) means "presence, arrival, coming" — a technical term used for the official visit of a king or dignitary. Synteleia (G4930) means "joint completion, consummation" — not merely "end" but the full bringing-together of all things to their appointed conclusion. The aion (G165) here denotes "the age," the present era of history.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And Jesus, answering, said to them, "See that no one leads you astray."

verses.flow: "For many shall come in my name, saying, 'I am the Christ,' and shall lead many astray."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jesus warns that false messianic claimants will arise "in my name" (epi tō onomati mou), claiming the unique title ho Christos — "the Christ," the definite article marking the one Anointed King of Israel. HISTORICAL: Multiple false messiahs arose in the first century, including Theudas (Acts 5:36), the Egyptian (Acts 21:38), and Simon bar Kokhba in AD 132.

verses.flow: "And you shall be about to hear of wars and rumors of wars. See that you are not troubled, for it is necessary for these things to come to pass, but the end is not yet."
"For nation shall be raised against nation, and kingdom against kingdom, and there shall be famines and earthquakes in various places."
"But all these things are the beginning of birth pains."
verses.note: GREEK: Ōdinōn (G5604) means "birth pains, labor pangs." This is a deliberate metaphor drawn from Old Testament prophetic imagery (Isa 13:8; 26:17; Jer 22:23). The birth-pain metaphor implies that these sufferings are not purposeless but are bringing forth something — the messianic age. TYPOLOGY: The prophets consistently used labor imagery for the travail preceding God's eschatological deliverance (Mic 4:9-10).

verses.flow: "Then they shall deliver you up to tribulation and shall kill you, and you shall be hated by all the nations on account of my name."
"And then many shall be caused to stumble, and shall betray one another, and shall hate one another."
"And many false prophets shall be raised up and shall lead many astray."
"And because lawlessness shall be multiplied, the love of the many shall grow cold."

verses.flow: "But the one having endured to the end, this one shall be saved."
"And this gospel of the kingdom shall be proclaimed in all the inhabited earth for a testimony to all the nations, and then the end shall come."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The gospel (euangelion, G2098) is specifically identified as "of the kingdom" (tēs basileias) — the proclamation that God's reign has been inaugurated through Christ the King. The scope is universal: the whole "inhabited earth" (oikoumenē, G3625), a term used in the Roman period for the civilized world. This verse establishes the global mission as a precondition for the end (telos).

verses.flow: "When therefore you see the abomination of desolation, the thing spoken through Daniel the prophet, standing in the holy place — let the one reading understand —"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "abomination of desolation" (bdelygma tēs erēmōseōs) directly references Daniel 9:27, 11:31, and 12:11 (LXX). Daniel's prophecy found a typological fulfillment in Antiochus IV Epiphanes' desecration of the temple in 167 BC (1 Macc 1:54), but Jesus points to a future and greater fulfillment. GREEK: Bdelygma (G946) denotes something utterly detestable, especially an idol or sacrilege. The parenthetical "let the one reading understand" (ho anaginōskōn noeitō) is likely an editorial note from Matthew drawing attention to Daniel's text.

verses.flow: "then let those in Judea flee to the mountains."
"Let the one upon the housetop not come down to take the things out of his house."
"And let the one in the field not turn back to take his garment."

verses.flow: "But woe to those having child in the womb and to those nursing in those days."
"And pray that your flight may not be in winter nor on a Sabbath."
"For then there shall be great tribulation, such as has not come to pass from the beginning of the world until now, nor ever shall come to pass."
verses.note: GREEK: Thlipsis megalē (G2347 + G3173) — "great tribulation" — echoes Daniel 12:1 (LXX), where Michael arises during a time of distress unparalleled in history. The double negative ou mē with the subjunctive genētai creates the strongest possible denial in Greek: such tribulation absolutely shall never occur again. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Daniel 12:1 places this tribulation in the context of angelic warfare, with Michael the great prince standing on behalf of God's people.

verses.flow: "And unless those days had been shortened, no flesh would be saved; but on account of the elect, those days shall be shortened."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb koloboō (G2856), "to cut short, amputate, shorten," appears only here and in the Markan parallel (Mark 13:20) in the NT. The elect (eklektous, G1588) are the cause of divine mercy shortening the period of catastrophe. This reveals God's sovereign governance over even the duration of tribulation — it proceeds only as far as his purposes require.

verses.flow: "Then if anyone says to you, 'Behold, here is the Christ,' or 'Here,' do not believe it."
"For false christs and false prophets shall be raised up, and they shall give great signs and wonders, so as to lead astray, if possible, even the elect."
"Behold, I have told you beforehand."
"If therefore they say to you, 'Behold, he is in the wilderness,' do not go out. 'Behold, he is in the inner rooms,' do not believe it."
"For just as the lightning comes forth from the east and shines as far as the west, so shall be the coming of the Son of Man."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The parousia of the Son of Man will not be a hidden or localized event requiring secret knowledge, but as unmistakable and universal as lightning (astrapē, G796) flashing across the entire sky from east to west. This directly counters the false claims of vv. 23-26. TYPOLOGY: "Son of Man" echoes Daniel 7:13-14, where the "one like a son of man" comes on the clouds to receive dominion from the Ancient of Days.

verses.flow: "For wherever the carcass may be, there the eagles shall be gathered together."

"And immediately after the tribulation of those days, the sun shall be darkened, and the moon shall not give its light, and the stars shall fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens shall be shaken."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse draws heavily on OT theophanic and judgment language. The darkening of sun and moon echoes Isaiah 13:10 and Joel 2:31. The falling of stars recalls Isaiah 34:4. The shaking of heavenly powers echoes Haggai 2:6. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "powers of the heavens" (hai dynameis tōn ouranōn) may refer both to cosmic phenomena and to spiritual powers in the heavenly realm (cf. Eph 6:12; Isa 24:21), where the host of heaven on high are punished alongside earthly rulers.

verses.flow: "And then the sign of the Son of Man shall appear in heaven, and then all the tribes of the earth shall mourn, and they shall see the Son of Man coming upon the clouds of heaven with power and great glory."
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Coming on the clouds" is a direct fulfillment of Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man receives from the Ancient of Days an everlasting kingdom, dominion, and glory. "All the tribes of the earth shall mourn" echoes Zechariah 12:10-14, where Israel mourns for the one they have pierced. GREEK: Kopsontai (G2875) literally means "to cut, strike the breast" in lamentation — a visceral mourning. The sign (sēmeion) of the Son of Man appearing in heaven declares his identity as the true and only King.

verses.flow: "And he shall send forth his angels with a great trumpet, and they shall gather together his elect from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other end of them."
verses.note: GREEK: Episynaxousin (G1996), "shall gather together," is a compound verb intensifying the gathering — a comprehensive, complete collection. TYPOLOGY: The gathering of the elect from the four winds echoes the prophetic ingathering of Israel's scattered people (Deut 30:4; Isa 27:13; Zech 2:6). The great trumpet (salpingos megalēs) recalls the shofar of Isaiah 27:13 and the trumpet of God in 1 Thessalonians 4:16.

verses.flow: "Now from the fig tree learn the parable: when already its branch becomes tender and puts forth its leaves, you know that the summer is near."
"So also you, when you see all these things, know that he is near, at the doors."
"Truly I say to you, this generation shall not pass away until all these things come to pass."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is one of the most debated in the NT. Genea (G1074) can mean "generation" (those living at a given time), "race/people," or "age/epoch." Some interpret this as Jesus' contemporaries who would witness the destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70; others see it as the Jewish people who will endure until the consummation; still others as the generation alive when eschatological signs begin. The phrase "all these things" (panta tauta) is the key interpretive question — does it refer to the temple's destruction, the parousia, or both?

verses.flow: "Heaven and earth shall pass away, but my words shall by no means pass away."
"But concerning that day and hour, no one knows, neither the angels of the heavens, nor the Son, except the Father alone."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Son's voluntary limitation of knowledge regarding the day and hour is a profound statement about the incarnation. Even the angels of the heavens — members of the divine council — do not possess this knowledge. It is reserved to the Father alone (ho patēr monos). DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse reveals a hierarchy of knowledge within the heavenly realm: angels do not know, the incarnate Son defers to the Father, and the Father alone holds the appointed time.

verses.flow: "For just as the days of Noah, so shall be the coming of the Son of Man."

verses.flow: "Two women grinding at the mill; one is taken and one is left."
"Watch therefore, for you do not know on what day your Lord comes."
verses.note: GREEK: Grēgoreite (G1127), "watch, be vigilant, stay awake," is a present imperative demanding continuous watchfulness. This becomes a key ethical imperative of early Christianity (1 Thess 5:6; 1 Pet 5:8; Rev 3:2-3). THEOLOGICAL: The uncertainty of the timing is not a cause for anxiety but for readiness — the appropriate posture of the servant awaiting the master's return.

verses.flow: "But know this, that if the master of the house had known in what watch the thief comes, he would have watched and would not have allowed his house to be broken into."
"Because of this, you also be ready, for in an hour you do not expect, the Son of Man comes."
"Who then is the faithful and wise servant, whom the lord set over his household, to give them food in due season?"
verses.note: GREEK: Pistos (G4103), "faithful, trustworthy," and phronimos (G5429), "prudent, wise, sensible" — these are the two qualities marking the ideal servant-steward. Oiketeia (G3610) refers to the household staff, the domestic servants. The faithful servant is defined not by knowledge of timing but by faithful discharge of assigned duties. TYPOLOGY: This parable anticipates the fuller parables of readiness in chapter 25 (virgins, talents).

verses.note: GREEK: Dichotomēsei (G1371), "shall cut in two, cut asunder" — an extreme and vivid term, possibly metaphorical for utter destruction or referring to an actual ancient punishment of dismemberment. THEOLOGICAL: The wicked servant's portion is set with the hypocrites (hypokritōn, G5273) — those whose outward appearance masks inner corruption. The phrase "the weeping and the gnashing of teeth" (ho klauthmos kai ho brygmos tōn odontōn) is a recurring Matthean formula for eschatological judgment (Matt 8:12; 13:42, 50; 22:13; 25:30), denoting irreversible anguish and rage.

verses.flow: Then the kingdom of the heavens shall be likened to ten virgins, who, having taken their lamps, went out to a meeting of the bridegroom.
verses.note: GREEK: "Homoiōthēsetai" (ὁμοιωθήσεται) is future passive of homoioō, "shall be likened," indicating a comparison that will become evident at the eschaton. "Lampadas" (λαμπάδας) refers to torches or oil-fed lamps, not candles. TYPOLOGY: The bridegroom figure echoes the OT portrayal of God as husband to Israel (Isa 54:5; Hos 2:16), here applied directly to Christ, the messianic bridegroom. "Hypantēsis" (ὑπάντησις) is a technical term for the formal reception of a dignitary arriving at a city (cf. 1 Thess 4:17).

verses.flow: And while the bridegroom delayed, they all grew drowsy and were sleeping.
verses.note: GREEK: "Chronízontos" (χρονίζοντος) is a genitive absolute participle from chronizō, meaning "to delay, to take a long time" — emphasizing the unexpected length of the bridegroom's absence. "Enystaxan" (ἐνύσταξαν, from nystazō) means to become drowsy or nod off, a different word than "katheudō" (to sleep deeply) which follows, showing a progressive falling asleep. THEOLOGICAL: Both wise and foolish fall asleep — the parable's point is not wakefulness but preparedness.

verses.flow: And while they were going away to buy, the bridegroom came, and those who were ready entered with him into the wedding feast, and the door was shut.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ekleisthē hē thyra" (ἐκλείσθη ἡ θύρα) — "the door was shut" — is an aorist passive indicating a decisive, irreversible divine action. This echoes Gen 7:16 where God shuts Noah into the ark. The shut door marks the absolute boundary between salvation and judgment. TYPOLOGY: Entry into the wedding feast prefigures the messianic banquet (Isa 25:6-8; Rev 19:9).

This verse serves as the hinge summarizing the parable's application and transitioning to the next parable. The exhortation to watchfulness is a repeated theme in the Olivet Discourse (Matt 24:42, 44).

verses.flow: For it is as a man going on a journey, who called his own servants and delivered to them his possessions.

verses.note: GREEK: "Talanton" (τάλαντον) was a unit of weight and monetary value — one talent equaled approximately 6,000 denarii, or roughly twenty years' wages for a laborer. "Kata tēn idian dynamin" (κατὰ τὴν ἰδίαν δύναμιν) — "according to his own ability" — indicates the master's sovereign, discerning distribution. HISTORICAL: In the Roman economy, large-scale financial stewardship was commonly entrusted to trusted household slaves (oikonomoi).

verses.flow: And cast the worthless servant into the outer darkness; there shall be the weeping and the gnashing of teeth.

"Throne of his glory" (θρόνου δόξης αὐτοῦ) parallels the throne of God himself (Isa 6:1; Ezek 1:26).

verses.note: GREEK: "Kolasin aiōnion" (κόλασιν αἰώνιον) — "eternal punishment" — uses "kolasis" (from kolazō, to prune or punish), a term emphasizing punitive consequence. "Aiōnios" (αἰώνιον) is applied identically to both punishment and life, indicating the same duration and quality of permanence for each. THEOLOGICAL: This verse forms the climactic conclusion to the entire Olivet Discourse (Matt 24-25). The parallel structure — "these into eternal punishment, but the righteous into eternal life" — presents the final, irrevocable bifurcation of humanity at the last judgment. "Zōēn aiōnion" (ζωὴν αἰώνιον) echoes Dan 12:2.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: σὺ εἶπας ("You have said it") is an affirmative response in Semitic idiom. Jesus then immediately escalates his claim by combining Daniel 7:13 (the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven) with Psalm 110:1 (sitting at the right hand of Power). Jesus claims the seat at the right hand of God's own throne and the authority of the heavenly Son of Man who receives an everlasting kingdom from the Ancient of Days (Dan 7:13–14). This is the definitive self-revelation that precipitates the charge of blasphemy.

verses.flow: And Jesus stood before the governor, and the governor questioned him, saying, "Are you the King of the Jews?" And Jesus said, "You say so."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The question "Are you the King of the Jews?" frames the entire passion narrative in royal terms. Jesus's reply σὺ λέγεις (sy legeis, "You say so") is an affirmative that redirects authority — Pilate speaks a truth beyond his own comprehension. Jesus is indeed the King, though his kingdom is not of this world's order (John 18:36). The charge that condemns him is, ironically, his truest identity.
